reagents.name: Sodium borohydride
conditions: Methanol, ethanol or aqueous alcohol
Protonation during work-up
reagents.scope: Best for aldehydes and ketones in teaching-lab contexts.
reagents.mechanism: Hydride delivery to the carbonyl carbon, followed by alkoxide protonation.
traps: Milder than LiAlH4
Usually does not reduce esters, acids or amides under standard conditions
reagents.safety: Use normal teaching-lab controls and consult the SDS before handling.

reagents.name: Lithium aluminium hydride
conditions: Dry ether before work-up
Careful acidic work-up after reduction
reagents.scope: Useful when NaBH4 is not strong enough.
reagents.mechanism: Powerful hydride transfer with moisture-sensitive aluminium hydride species.
traps: Destroyed by water
Can reduce more functional groups than NaBH4
reagents.safety: Moisture-sensitive reducing agent. Follow institutional risk assessment.

reagents.name: Pyridinium chlorochromate
conditions: Anhydrous solvent such as CH2Cl2 in many textbook examples
Avoid excess water when aldehyde is desired
reagents.scope: Selective oxidation when over-oxidation is a concern.
reagents.mechanism: Chromate ester formation followed by elimination to the carbonyl.
traps: Acidified dichromate under reflux often over-oxidizes primary alcohols
PCC is commonly used to stop at the aldehyde
reagents.safety: Chromium(VI) reagent. Treat as hazardous and follow waste rules.

reagents.name: Potassium manganate(VII)
transformations: Cold dilute conditions can form vicinal diols
Hot concentrated conditions can cleave alkenes
Primary alcohols can oxidize to carboxylic acids
conditions: Cold dilute alkaline conditions for dihydroxylation
Hot concentrated conditions for harsher oxidation
reagents.scope: Condition-dependent oxidizer with useful qualitative and synthetic contexts.
reagents.mechanism: Manganate ester formation and oxidation state changes at manganese.
traps: Cold dilute and hot concentrated conditions give different outcomes
A purple-to-brown change is a common qualitative clue
reagents.safety: Strong oxidizer. Keep away from incompatible organic materials unless instructed.

reagents.name: Catalytic hydrogenation
transformations: Alkyne to alkane under full hydrogenation
conditions: Hydrogen gas with palladium catalyst
Controlled conditions depending on substrate
reagents.scope: Reduces carbon-carbon multiple bonds and sometimes other reducible groups.
reagents.mechanism: Adsorption on metal surface, syn hydrogen delivery, product desorption.
traps: Removes unsaturation rather than replacing a leaving group
Catalyst poisoning can matter in advanced contexts
reagents.safety: Hydrogen and supported metal catalysts require proper controls.

reagents.name: Hydrogen bromide addition
transformations: Anti-Markovnikov product with peroxide radical conditions
conditions: HBr at room temperature for ionic addition
Peroxides change the pathway in common teaching examples
reagents.scope: Useful for showing carbocation stability and regioselectivity.
reagents.mechanism: Ionic electrophilic addition, or radical chain addition with peroxides.
traps: Peroxide effect is mainly taught for HBr
Carbocation rearrangements may appear in advanced questions
reagents.safety: Corrosive acid gas or solution. Use only under approved controls.

reagents.name: Bromine
conditions: Inert solvent for addition
Bromine water for qualitative tests
reagents.scope: Common diagnostic reagent for C=C and a mechanistic example of anti-addition.
reagents.mechanism: Cyclic bromonium ion formation followed by backside bromide attack.
traps: Decolourisation supports unsaturation but is not a complete structure proof
Benzene bromination needs a catalyst
reagents.safety: Toxic and corrosive. Follow strict handling and disposal rules.

reagents.name: Bromination of benzene
transformations: Activated rings brominate faster than benzene
conditions: Bromine with FeBr3 or AlBr3 catalyst
reagents.scope: Classic electrophilic aromatic substitution example.
reagents.mechanism: Lewis acid polarizes bromine to generate a stronger electrophile; aromaticity is restored after deprotonation.
traps: Benzene needs activation of bromine
Substituents direct ortho/para or meta in substituted rings
reagents.safety: Uses corrosive bromine and Lewis acid catalyst. Educational reference only.

reagents.name: Thionyl chloride
conditions: Dry conditions are often used
Base such as pyridine may be present in some examples
reagents.scope: Converts poor leaving groups into better chloride-containing products.
reagents.mechanism: Formation of chlorosulfite or acyl chlorosulfite intermediate, then substitution.
traps: Gaseous by-products can help drive reactions
Moisture can hydrolyze acid chlorides
reagents.safety: Releases acidic gases. Requires appropriate ventilation and controls.

reagents.name: Grignard reagent
transformations: CO2 to carboxylic acid after work-up
conditions: Dry ether before addition
Acidic work-up after carbon-carbon bond formation
reagents.scope: Important carbon nucleophile for building molecular skeletons.
reagents.mechanism: Polar organomagnesium bond behaves as a carbanion-like nucleophile.
traps: Destroyed by water, alcohols and acids
Formaldehyde gives primary alcohols; other aldehydes give secondary alcohols
reagents.safety: Moisture-sensitive organometallic reagent. Follow approved protocols.

reagents.name: Friedel-Crafts acylation
conditions: Acyl chloride and AlCl3
Anhydrous conditions in textbook examples
reagents.scope: Adds an acyl group without the same rearrangement issue as alkylation.
reagents.mechanism: Acylium ion formation, aromatic attack, deprotonation and catalyst release.
traps: Acyl groups are deactivating
The product is less reactive toward further acylation
reagents.safety: Lewis acids and acyl chlorides are moisture-sensitive and corrosive.

reagents.name: Diels-Alder reaction
transformations: Conjugated diene plus alkene or alkyne to cyclohexene framework
conditions: Thermal activation in many textbook examples
Electron-rich diene and electron-poor dienophile are favoured
reagents.scope: Powerful route to cyclic structures with predictable stereochemical relationships.
reagents.mechanism: Concerted [4+2] cycloaddition with cyclic electron movement.
traps: Diene must adopt s-cis geometry
Stereochemistry of the dienophile is retained
reagents.safety: General reaction class. Follow specific reagent SDS.

reagents.name: Aryl diazonium coupling
transformations: Diazonium salt plus activated arene to azo compound
conditions: Cold diazotisation conditions
Mildly alkaline coupling with phenols in common examples
reagents.scope: Links aromatic rings through an azo group and explains dye chromophores.
reagents.mechanism: Activated arene attacks diazonium electrophile, followed by proton transfer.
traps: Temperature control matters for diazonium stability
Phenols and aromatic amines are strongly activating
reagents.safety: Some diazonium salts are unstable. Use only as supervised reference chemistry.

reagents.name: meta-Chloroperoxybenzoic acid
transformations: Ketone to ester or lactone under Baeyer-Villiger conditions
conditions: Often used in chlorinated or weakly polar organic solvent examples
Buffered conditions may be used to reduce acid-sensitive side reactions
reagents.scope: Useful for electrophilic oxygen transfer where stereochemical retention of alkene geometry matters.
reagents.mechanism: Concerted oxygen transfer to an alkene or migration-controlled insertion next to a carbonyl in Baeyer-Villiger chemistry.
traps: Peracids are oxidizers, not generic acid catalysts
Migratory aptitude matters in Baeyer-Villiger reactions
reagents.safety: Organic peroxide oxidizer. Use only with approved handling, storage and incompatibility controls.

reagents.name: N-Bromosuccinimide
transformations: Benzylic bromination in selected radical contexts
conditions: Radical initiator or light in many textbook examples
Low bromine concentration helps favour substitution over alkene addition
reagents.scope: A standard reagent for discussing radical selectivity at resonance-stabilised positions.
reagents.mechanism: Radical chain bromination through allylic radical formation and bromine transfer.
traps: Do not confuse allylic bromination with ionic bromine addition across a double bond
Mixtures can occur when multiple allylic sites are available
reagents.safety: Oxidizing brominating reagent. Consult SDS and institutional controls before handling.

reagents.name: Lithium diisopropylamide
conditions: Dry aprotic solvent and low temperature in common selectivity examples
Electrophile added after enolate formation
reagents.scope: Useful for kinetic deprotonation and enolate-controlled synthesis planning.
reagents.mechanism: Bulky strong base removes an alpha proton to form an enolate under conditions that can favour kinetic control.
traps: Kinetic and thermodynamic enolates are different claims
Moisture or protic impurities destroy the base
reagents.safety: Strong moisture-sensitive base. Follow approved inert-atmosphere risk assessment.

reagents.name: Wittig reagent
transformations: Carbonyl oxygen replaced by C=C fragment
conditions: Phosphonium ylide generation before carbonyl addition
Stereochemical outcome depends on ylide class and conditions
reagents.scope: Important olefination strategy for building substituted alkenes from carbonyl precursors.
reagents.mechanism: Ylide addition to carbonyl followed by oxaphosphetane formation and collapse to alkene plus phosphine oxide.
traps: Stabilised and unstabilised ylides can show different stereochemical preferences
The carbonyl carbon becomes one alkene carbon
reagents.safety: Use reagent-specific SDS and waste rules for phosphine-derived materials.

reagents.name: Swern oxidation
conditions: Low temperature activation of DMSO
Base addition after alkoxysulfonium formation
reagents.scope: A chromium-free oxidation logic often discussed for sensitive substrates.
reagents.mechanism: Activated DMSO forms an alkoxysulfonium intermediate followed by base-induced elimination to carbonyl.
traps: Temperature control and addition order are part of the method claim
By-products and odour are practical considerations
reagents.safety: Uses corrosive activation reagents and volatile by-products. Requires appropriate controls.

reagents.name: Ozonolysis
transformations: Alkene to aldehydes or ketones under reductive work-up
Alkene to acids or ketones under oxidative work-up
conditions: Ozone generation followed by specified reductive or oxidative work-up
Low-temperature solvent conditions in many examples
reagents.scope: Useful for locating double bonds and planning carbonyl fragments.
reagents.mechanism: Cycloaddition to ozonide-derived intermediates followed by work-up-dependent cleavage products.
traps: Work-up changes the oxidation state of products
Product fragments must be mapped back to the original alkene
reagents.safety: Ozone is a powerful oxidizer and respiratory hazard. Educational reference only.

reagents.name: Potassium hydroxide
reagents.focus: Strong base and carbon activation reagent
transformations: Carbon activation in porous carbon preparation
conditions: Aqueous or alcoholic base depending on substrate
High-temperature activation contexts are materials-specific
reagents.scope: Important strong base in synthesis, analytical preparation and activated carbon production discussions.
reagents.mechanism: Hydroxide acts as nucleophile or base; in carbon activation, chemical etching and gas evolution develop porosity.
traps: Aqueous base chemistry and high-temperature carbon activation are different method contexts
Strong base can destroy sensitive functional groups
reagents.safety: Strong corrosive base. Follow approved caustic handling controls.

reagents.name: Dess-Martin periodinane
conditions: Neutral or mildly acidic organic media in many examples
Water-sensitive substrates may benefit from mild conditions
reagents.scope: A useful chromium-free oxidation reagent for sensitive alcohol substrates.
reagents.mechanism: Ligand exchange to iodine followed by elimination gives the carbonyl product.
traps: Do not treat all iodine(V) oxidants as interchangeable
Reaction work-up and reagent age can affect performance
reagents.safety: Hypervalent iodine oxidant. Follow reagent-specific SDS, incompatibility and waste guidance.

reagents.name: TEMPO oxidation system
transformations: Primary alcohol to aldehyde or acid depending on co-oxidant
conditions: TEMPO with a terminal oxidant such as bleach or related systems
pH and co-oxidant determine selectivity
reagents.scope: Important for discussing catalytic oxidation and greener replacement logic.
reagents.mechanism: Nitroxyl radical cycles through oxoammonium species that accept hydride-equivalent from alcohol.
traps: The terminal oxidant is part of the method claim
Aldehyde versus acid selectivity requires condition context
reagents.safety: Oxidant system. Consult SDS for both TEMPO and the terminal oxidant.

reagents.name: Jones reagent
conditions: Aqueous acidic conditions
Often discussed as a strong oxidation contrast to PCC or DMP
reagents.scope: A historically important oxidant for robust substrates where over-oxidation is not a concern.
reagents.mechanism: Chromate ester formation followed by oxidation and hydrolysis pathways.
traps: Primary alcohols generally do not stop at aldehyde under aqueous acidic conditions
Chromium waste dominates sustainability assessment
reagents.safety: Chromium(VI), strongly oxidizing and hazardous. Educational reference only unless institutionally approved.

reagents.name: Osmium tetroxide
conditions: Often catalytic with terminal reoxidant
Co-oxidant and ligand system define the procedure
reagents.scope: A high-value stereochemical reagent for syn addition across alkenes.
reagents.mechanism: Concerted cycloaddition to osmate ester followed by hydrolysis gives syn diol.
traps: Contrast with anti dihydroxylation through epoxide opening
Catalytic variants still carry serious hazard constraints
reagents.safety: Highly toxic volatile oxidant. Treat as a specialist controlled reagent.

reagents.name: Lindlar catalyst
conditions: Hydrogen gas with poisoned palladium catalyst
Careful control avoids full hydrogenation
reagents.scope: Useful for stopping alkyne reduction at the alkene stage.
reagents.mechanism: Surface hydrogen delivery is moderated by catalyst poisoning, favouring syn partial reduction.
traps: Ordinary Pd/C may over-reduce to alkane
Product geometry differs from dissolving-metal reduction
reagents.safety: Hydrogenation system. Follow approved gas and catalyst controls.

reagents.name: Birch reduction
conditions: Alkali metal in liquid ammonia with proton source
Substituent electronics influence regiochemistry
reagents.scope: Advanced reagent system for partial arene reduction and mechanistic radical-anion discussion.
reagents.mechanism: Electron transfer, protonation and further electron/proton transfer produce non-aromatic diene.
traps: Regiochemistry depends on substituent class
This is not catalytic hydrogenation
reagents.safety: Cryogenic ammonia and alkali metal conditions are specialist hazards. Educational reference.

reagents.name: Diisobutylaluminium hydride
transformations: Ester to aldehyde under controlled low-temperature conditions
conditions: Low temperature and stoichiometry control are central
Dry conditions before work-up
reagents.scope: Useful when an ester-to-aldehyde disconnection is required.
reagents.mechanism: Bulky aluminium hydride forms reducible aluminium intermediate; work-up releases aldehyde under controlled conditions.
traps: Over-reduction can occur if conditions are not controlled
Low temperature is part of the selectivity claim
reagents.safety: Pyrophoric or moisture-sensitive hydride solution. Specialist handling required.

reagents.name: Sodium bis(2-methoxyethoxy)aluminium hydride
transformations: Esters and carboxylic derivatives to alcohols
Amides to amines in selected procedures
conditions: Anhydrous solvent conditions
Procedure-specific temperature and quench control
reagents.scope: A more soluble aluminium hydride alternative in some synthetic contexts.
reagents.mechanism: Aluminium hydride transfer to electrophilic carbonyl derivatives followed by work-up.
traps: Functional-group tolerance is not the same as NaBH4
Quench safety dominates operational design
reagents.safety: Moisture-sensitive reducing agent. Use strict institutional controls.

reagents.name: Raney nickel
conditions: Hydrogen atmosphere or hydrogen-transfer conditions depending on method
Wet catalyst handling is critical
reagents.scope: Useful catalyst class for reduction and C-S bond removal discussions.
reagents.mechanism: Metal surface activates hydrogen and substrate; desulfurization involves strong sulfur-nickel interactions.
traps: Dry Raney nickel can be pyrophoric
Catalyst poisoning and substrate adsorption strongly affect outcomes
reagents.safety: Nickel catalyst with pyrophoric risk if dry. Follow catalyst storage and disposal procedures.

reagents.name: Sodium cyanoborohydride
transformations: Imine or iminium ion to amine
Aldehyde or ketone plus amine to substituted amine
conditions: Mildly acidic conditions often support iminium formation
Avoid strongly acidic conditions that release hazardous gases
reagents.scope: A classic selective hydride source for reductive amination logic.
reagents.mechanism: Imine formation followed by hydride transfer to iminium carbon.
traps: pH affects both imine formation and reagent safety
Direct carbonyl reduction competes under some conditions
reagents.safety: Cyanoborohydride reagent. Avoid acid misuse and consult SDS.

reagents.name: Sodium triacetoxyborohydride
transformations: Aldehyde or ketone plus amine to amine product
conditions: Mild organic acid conditions in many examples
Often used when cyanoborohydride risk is undesirable
reagents.scope: Useful selective reagent for iminium reduction in medicinal chemistry style planning.
reagents.mechanism: Acetoxyborohydride transfers hydride to iminium species under mild conditions.
traps: Water content and acid strength can change performance
Ketones can be slower than aldehydes
reagents.safety: Hydride reagent. Follow SDS and avoid incompatible quench conditions.

reagents.name: Borane tetrahydrofuran complex
transformations: Alkene hydroboration followed by oxidation to alcohol
conditions: Anhydrous ether solvent
Oxidative work-up for hydroboration-oxidation sequences
reagents.scope: Valuable for selective carboxylic acid reduction and anti-Markovnikov alcohol synthesis logic.
reagents.mechanism: Lewis acidic borane activates heteroatom-containing substrates or adds concertedly across alkenes.
traps: BH3 reduction and hydroboration are different contexts
Work-up defines final hydroboration product
reagents.safety: Moisture-sensitive borane complex. Follow inert-atmosphere controls.

reagents.name: 9-Borabicyclo[3.3.1]nonane
transformations: Terminal alkene to primary alcohol after oxidation
conditions: Anhydrous conditions before oxidative work-up
Steric selectivity can improve terminal alkene preference
reagents.scope: A bulky hydroboration reagent for selectivity-driven alkene functionalization.
reagents.mechanism: Concerted syn addition of B-H across alkene followed by oxidative replacement of C-B with C-O.
traps: Regiochemistry is anti-Markovnikov but stereochemistry is syn addition
Oxidation work-up is required for alcohol product
reagents.safety: Air- and moisture-sensitive organoborane reagent. Follow SDS.

reagents.name: Alkaline hydrogen peroxide
transformations: Alkene hydroboration product to anti-Markovnikov alcohol
conditions: Basic peroxide work-up after hydroboration
Temperature and addition control limit side reactions
reagents.scope: Essential work-up reagent for hydroboration-oxidation sequences.
reagents.mechanism: Peroxide attacks boron followed by migration and hydrolysis to replace C-B with C-O.
traps: Not a standalone alkene hydration reagent without prior hydroboration
Peroxide concentration and compatibility matter
reagents.safety: Oxidizing peroxide/base mixture. Follow approved controls.

reagents.name: Phosphorus tribromide
transformations: Primary or secondary alcohol to alkyl bromide
conditions: Anhydrous conditions commonly used
Base or controlled addition may be used depending on procedure
reagents.scope: Useful for converting alcohols into better leaving groups with SN2-like inversion logic.
reagents.mechanism: Alcohol forms phosphite ester, then bromide displacement gives alkyl bromide.
traps: Tertiary alcohols are not the standard SN2 case
Stereochemical inversion depends on substrate and pathway
reagents.safety: Corrosive, moisture-sensitive phosphorus reagent. Releases acidic by-products.

reagents.name: p-Toluenesulfonyl chloride
transformations: Poor leaving group to activated sulfonate ester
conditions: Base such as pyridine or amine in common examples
Water exclusion protects sulfonyl chloride
reagents.scope: Allows alcohol activation without breaking the C-O bond at the stereocentre.
reagents.mechanism: Alcohol oxygen attacks sulfur, chloride leaves, base removes proton to form sulfonate ester.
traps: Tosylation retains configuration at carbon; later substitution may invert
Activation is not itself nucleophilic replacement
reagents.safety: Sulfonyl chloride irritant/corrosive profile. Use approved controls.

reagents.name: Methanesulfonyl chloride
conditions: Amine base and controlled temperature in many examples
Dry conditions protect the reagent
reagents.scope: Compact sulfonyl activation reagent for substitution and elimination planning.
reagents.mechanism: Sulfonyl chloride reacts with alcohol oxygen to form sulfonate ester.
traps: Mesylate formation is distinct from C-O bond substitution
Base choice can affect elimination in later steps
reagents.safety: Reactive sulfonyl chloride. Follow corrosive reagent handling rules.

reagents.name: Diphenyl phosphoryl azide
reagents.focus: Curtius rearrangement and acyl azide chemistry
conditions: Base-assisted activation followed by thermal rearrangement in selected procedures
Nucleophile trapping defines product
reagents.scope: Advanced reagent for one-carbon-deletion amine or carbamate synthesis planning.
reagents.mechanism: Acyl azide formation followed by nitrogen extrusion and rearrangement to isocyanate.
traps: Azide safety and temperature control are part of the method claim
Product depends on trapping nucleophile
reagents.safety: Azide reagent. Specialist risk assessment required.

reagents.name: Diethyl azodicarboxylate
transformations: Alcohol plus pronucleophile to substituted product with inversion
conditions: Triphenylphosphine and acidic pronucleophile in common examples
Substrate acidity and sterics control viability
reagents.scope: Important for stereochemical inversion and alcohol substitution under mild conditions.
reagents.mechanism: Phosphine activates azo reagent and alcohol, enabling displacement by conjugate base of pronucleophile.
traps: Pronucleophile pKa matters
Inversion is expected at suitable stereogenic alcohol centers
reagents.safety: Azo reagent. Follow reagent SDS and avoid incompatible reducing conditions.

reagents.name: Triphenylphosphine
transformations: Reduces azides to amines in Staudinger chemistry
conditions: Often used with electrophilic or oxidizing partners
Phosphine oxide by-product is common
reagents.scope: Versatile reagent and ligand for phosphorus-mediated transformations.
reagents.mechanism: Nucleophilic phosphorus forms phosphonium intermediates or coordinates metals as ligand.
traps: Phosphine oxide removal can dominate purification
Role changes by reaction context
reagents.safety: Irritant organic phosphine. Follow SDS and ventilation guidance.

reagents.name: N,N'-Dicyclohexylcarbodiimide
transformations: Carboxylic acid plus amine to amide
Carboxylic acid plus alcohol to ester
conditions: Often combined with additives to suppress side reactions
Insoluble dicyclohexylurea by-product forms
reagents.scope: Classic dehydrative coupling reagent for amide and ester bond formation.
reagents.mechanism: Carbodiimide activates acid as O-acylisourea, then nucleophile attacks acyl carbon.
traps: O-acylisourea rearrangement can reduce yield
Allergic sensitization risk is important operationally
reagents.safety: Sensitizer and irritant. Use strict exposure controls.

reagents.name: 1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide
transformations: Carboxylic acid plus amine to amide
Surface or biomolecule coupling with additives
conditions: Often used with NHS or sulfo-NHS additives
pH controls carboxylate activation and amine nucleophilicity
reagents.scope: Widely used in bioconjugation and materials surface functionalization.
reagents.mechanism: Water-soluble carbodiimide activates carboxyl groups toward nucleophilic substitution.
traps: Hydrolysis competes in water
pH and buffer compatibility are not optional details
reagents.safety: Irritant coupling reagent. Follow SDS and avoid skin exposure.

reagents.name: HATU
transformations: Carboxylic acid plus amine to amide
conditions: Base such as DIPEA in polar aprotic solvent in many examples
Stoichiometry and racemization control matter
reagents.scope: High-activity coupling reagent for demanding amide bond formation.
reagents.mechanism: Uronium activation creates reactive acyl intermediate for amine attack.
traps: Epimerization and overactivation remain possible
Not all amide couplings need high-activity reagent
reagents.safety: Coupling reagent with irritant profile. Follow SDS.

reagents.name: 4-Dimethylaminopyridine
conditions: Used catalytically with acid chlorides or anhydrides
Base and solvent affect rate and selectivity
reagents.scope: Important organocatalyst for acylation and esterification contexts.
reagents.mechanism: DMAP forms a reactive acylpyridinium intermediate that is attacked by nucleophile.
traps: Catalytic amount can have large rate effect
Nucleophilic catalysis differs from simple base catalysis
reagents.safety: Toxic/irritant organic base. Avoid exposure.

reagents.name: Pyridine
transformations: Base support for sulfonylation and chlorination
conditions: Used as solvent or stoichiometric base in many examples
Odour and toxicity influence practical selection
reagents.scope: General organic base and nucleophilic mediator in acylation chemistry.
reagents.mechanism: Pyridine accepts proton or forms acylpyridinium species depending on reagent context.
traps: Base role and nucleophilic catalyst role should be separated
Stronger or less nucleophilic bases may be preferred
reagents.safety: Flammable toxic liquid. Follow ventilation and exposure controls.

reagents.name: N,N-Diisopropylethylamine
conditions: Common in peptide coupling with uronium reagents
Used where lower nucleophilicity is desired
reagents.scope: Hindered amine base for activation chemistry and salt formation control.
reagents.mechanism: Acts primarily as proton acceptor while limiting nucleophilic side reactions.
traps: Not a universal non-nucleophilic base
Stoichiometry affects salt load and work-up
reagents.safety: Volatile amine base. Use ventilation and SDS guidance.

reagents.name: Potassium tert-butoxide
conditions: Dry alcohol or aprotic solvents depending on procedure
Steric bulk influences regioselectivity
reagents.scope: Useful for favouring elimination and sometimes less substituted alkene products.
reagents.mechanism: Strong base abstracts beta hydrogen while leaving group departs in E2 contexts.
traps: Bulky base can change Zaitsev/Hofmann outcome
Substitution can still compete for some substrates
reagents.safety: Strong moisture-sensitive base. Handle with strict dry controls.

reagents.name: Sodium hydride
transformations: Acidic C-H or N-H deprotonation in selected contexts
conditions: Dry aprotic solvent
Hydrogen gas evolution requires control
reagents.scope: Common base for generating alkoxides and anions for substitution or alkylation.
reagents.mechanism: Hydride acts as strong base toward protic sites, releasing hydrogen.
traps: NaH is generally a base, not a hydride reducing agent in typical organic synthesis use
Gas evolution and mineral oil dispersion matter
reagents.safety: Water-reactive base with hydrogen evolution. Follow strict controls.

reagents.name: Trimethylsilyl chloride
transformations: Activation of oxygenated substrates in selected contexts
conditions: Base or imidazole-type additive in many examples
Moisture exclusion improves efficiency
reagents.scope: Entry silylation reagent for protection and analytical derivatization concepts.
reagents.mechanism: Oxygen attacks silicon and chloride leaves, with base removing proton.
traps: TMS ethers are relatively labile compared with bulkier silyl ethers
Moisture reverses or competes with silylation
reagents.safety: Moisture-sensitive chlorosilane. Releases acidic by-products.

reagents.name: tert-Butyldimethylsilyl chloride
conditions: Imidazole or amine base in polar aprotic solvent
Steric environment controls selectivity
reagents.scope: Widely used protecting group for alcohols in multistep planning.
reagents.mechanism: Alcohol oxygen attacks silicon; chloride leaves and base traps acid.
traps: Primary alcohols are often protected faster than hindered alcohols
Deprotection conditions should be planned before installation
reagents.safety: Reactive chlorosilane. Follow SDS and moisture controls.

reagents.name: Tetrabutylammonium fluoride
conditions: Fluoride source in polar organic solvent
Water content and substrate sensitivity matter
reagents.scope: Standard reagent for removing many silyl protecting groups.
reagents.mechanism: Fluoride attacks silicon, breaking the Si-O bond and releasing alcohol.
traps: Fluoride basicity/nucleophilicity can affect sensitive substrates
Not all silyl groups cleave at the same rate
reagents.safety: Fluoride-containing reagent. Follow SDS and avoid exposure.

reagents.name: Di-tert-butyl dicarbonate
conditions: Base or nucleophilic catalyst often used
Aqueous-organic or organic conditions depending on substrate
reagents.scope: Common protecting reagent for amines in synthesis and medicinal chemistry.
reagents.mechanism: Amine attacks activated carbonate, releasing tert-butoxycarbonyl-protected product.
traps: Boc protection changes basicity and solubility
Acid-labile nature must match downstream steps
reagents.safety: Irritant reagent. Follow SDS.

reagents.name: Trifluoroacetic acid
conditions: Often used neat or in dichloromethane-type mixtures
Scavengers may be needed for sensitive substrates
reagents.scope: Strong organic acid for deprotection and peptide chemistry contexts.
reagents.mechanism: Acid protonates carbamate and drives fragmentation to amine salt, carbon dioxide and tert-butyl cation-derived products.
traps: Strong acid can also affect other acid-labile groups
Volatility and corrosivity matter operationally
reagents.safety: Corrosive volatile acid. Use appropriate ventilation and PPE.

reagents.name: Ninhydrin
transformations: Amino acid or amine to coloured chromophore
conditions: Spray or solution detection followed by heating in many examples
Response depends on amine class
reagents.scope: Classic visualization reagent for amino acids and amines.
reagents.mechanism: Oxidative deamination and chromophore formation produce Ruhemann-type colour.
traps: Colour response is diagnostic but not a complete identity proof
Secondary amines can behave differently
reagents.safety: Analytical reagent. Follow SDS and avoid inhalation of sprays.

reagents.name: 2,4-Dinitrophenylhydrazine
transformations: Aldehyde or ketone to hydrazone derivative
conditions: Acidic reagent mixture in qualitative testing
Derivative melting point historically used for identification
reagents.scope: Teaching-lab reagent for confirming aldehyde or ketone class.
reagents.mechanism: Hydrazine nucleophile adds to carbonyl followed by dehydration to hydrazone.
traps: Positive test indicates carbonyl class, not full structure
Reagent storage condition affects safety
reagents.safety: Dry DNPH can be hazardous. Use only stabilized reagent under approved controls.

reagents.name: Tollens reagent
conditions: Freshly prepared ammoniacal silver solution
Immediate disposal after use in teaching contexts
reagents.scope: Classical qualitative distinction for aldehydes versus many ketones.
reagents.mechanism: Aldehyde reduces silver complex while being oxidized under basic conditions.
traps: Alpha-hydroxy ketones may also respond
Old reagent residues can form hazardous silver compounds
reagents.safety: Prepare fresh and dispose immediately according to institutional rules.

reagents.name: Fehling's solution
transformations: Cu(II) reduced to Cu(I) oxide precipitate
conditions: Alkaline copper-tartrate solution with heating
Used as a qualitative redox test
reagents.scope: Classic test for reducing sugars and some aldehydes.
reagents.mechanism: Reducing substrate transfers electrons to Cu(II), forming Cu2O precipitate.
traps: Not a universal carbonyl test
Reducing sugars equilibrate to reactive aldehyde forms
reagents.safety: Copper-containing alkaline reagent. Follow waste rules.

reagents.name: Grubbs second-generation catalyst
conditions: Degassed organic solvent often used
Ethylene removal can drive some equilibria
reagents.scope: Important transition-metal catalyst for alkene exchange and polymer synthesis.
reagents.mechanism: Ruthenium carbene cycles through metallacyclobutane intermediates to exchange alkene fragments.
traps: Metathesis is equilibrium-controlled
Functional-group tolerance depends on catalyst generation
reagents.safety: Organometallic catalyst. Follow SDS and metal waste handling.

reagents.name: Tetrakis(triphenylphosphine)palladium(0)
conditions: Base and organoboron partner for Suzuki couplings
Air sensitivity varies with handling and substrate
reagents.scope: Canonical Pd(0) catalyst for oxidative addition/transmetalation/reductive elimination logic.
reagents.mechanism: Pd cycles through oxidative addition, transmetalation and reductive elimination.
traps: Catalyst, ligand, base and solvent are a coupled method system
Aryl chlorides are often harder than bromides or iodides
reagents.safety: Palladium complex. Follow metal catalyst handling and waste rules.

reagents.name: XPhos palladium precatalyst
conditions: Base, solvent and temperature selected for substrate class
Precatalyst activation generates active Pd species
reagents.scope: Advanced catalyst platform for demanding C-N and C-C bond formation.
reagents.mechanism: Bulky electron-rich phosphine supports oxidative addition and reductive elimination.
traps: Ligand identity is not decoration; it changes catalyst performance
Substrate class determines base choice
reagents.safety: Organometallic catalyst. Follow SDS and metal waste handling.

reagents.name: Copper(I) bromide
conditions: Ligand and redox balance define ATRP control
Oxygen removal often critical
reagents.scope: Important catalyst for controlled radical polymerization and materials synthesis.
reagents.mechanism: Cu(I)/Cu(II) redox cycle reversibly activates alkyl halide chain ends.
traps: Ligand, oxygen level and initiator ratio control polymer dispersity
Copper residue can affect material properties
reagents.safety: Copper salt. Follow SDS and waste rules.

reagents.name: Azobisisobutyronitrile
conditions: Controlled heating in compatible solvent or monomer
Initiator half-life determines radical flux
reagents.scope: Standard initiator for radical polymerization and mechanistic chain-growth discussion.
reagents.mechanism: Thermal N-N bond cleavage releases nitrogen and carbon-centered radicals.
traps: Initiator concentration affects molecular weight
Thermal decomposition and storage stability matter
reagents.safety: Thermally sensitive radical initiator. Follow storage and heating limits.

reagents.name: Benzoyl peroxide
transformations: Benzylic or allylic radical processes in selected contexts
conditions: Thermal decomposition under controlled conditions
Compatible monomer and inhibitor status matter
reagents.scope: Common organic peroxide initiator for polymerization and radical chemistry.
reagents.mechanism: O-O bond homolysis forms benzoyloxy radicals that initiate chain growth.
traps: Peroxide hazard is part of reagent identity
Inhibitors and oxygen alter polymerization behaviour
reagents.safety: Organic peroxide oxidizer. Observe strict storage, friction and heat controls.

reagents.name: Ammonium persulfate / TEMED
conditions: Aqueous monomer with crosslinker
TEMED accelerates radical formation
reagents.scope: Standard initiation system for electrophoresis gels and hydrogel contexts.
reagents.mechanism: Persulfate radical generation initiates vinyl polymerization; TEMED accelerates redox initiation.
traps: Oxygen inhibits radical polymerization
Acrylamide monomer toxicity is a separate material hazard
reagents.safety: Persulfate oxidizer and acrylamide systems require strict exposure controls.

reagents.name: Tetraethyl orthosilicate
conditions: Acidic or basic hydrolysis with water/alcohol
pH controls particle growth and network structure
reagents.scope: Key precursor for silica particles, coatings and hybrid sol-gel materials.
reagents.mechanism: Si-OR hydrolysis forms Si-OH followed by Si-O-Si condensation.
traps: Water ratio and pH are structural variables, not minor details
Gelation time depends strongly on conditions
reagents.safety: Flammable alkoxide precursor. Follow SDS and ventilation controls.

reagents.name: Titanium isopropoxide
transformations: Hydrolysis-condensation route to titania particles or films
conditions: Moisture-controlled hydrolysis
Chelating additives can moderate reaction rate
reagents.scope: Widely used precursor for TiO2 coatings, nanoparticles and photocatalytic material studies.
reagents.mechanism: Rapid hydrolysis produces Ti-OH species that condense into Ti-O-Ti networks.
traps: Uncontrolled water addition causes precipitation
Calcination history changes phase and surface area
reagents.safety: Moisture-sensitive flammable alkoxide. Follow dry handling controls.

reagents.name: Zinc acetate
transformations: Zinc salt to ZnO nanoparticles or films
conditions: Base, solvent and thermal treatment define morphology
Annealing controls crystallinity
reagents.scope: Accessible precursor for ZnO materials and transparent semiconductor discussions.
reagents.mechanism: Hydrolysis/precipitation forms zinc hydroxide or basic salts followed by dehydration to ZnO.
traps: Particle size, defect density and annealing history control properties
Salt precursor does not define final material alone
reagents.safety: Zinc salt. Follow SDS and disposal guidance.

reagents.name: Iron(II) sulfate
reagents.focus: Iron oxide precursor and redox salt
transformations: Iron salt to iron oxide or hydroxide particles
conditions: Base precipitation and oxidation state control
Inert or oxidative atmosphere changes products
reagents.scope: Useful precursor for magnetite-type materials and redox demonstrations.
reagents.mechanism: Hydroxide precipitation and oxidation/hydrolysis generate iron oxide phases.
traps: Fe(II) oxidizes readily
pH, oxygen and Fe(II):Fe(III) ratio define phase
reagents.safety: Iron salt. Follow SDS and avoid uncontrolled dust or waste release.

reagents.name: Graphite oxide
transformations: Oxidized graphite to graphene oxide dispersion
conditions: Oxidation history defines oxygen functionality
Exfoliation and reduction conditions control conductivity
reagents.scope: Important carbon material for membranes, composites and electrochemical interfaces.
reagents.mechanism: Oxidative functionalization introduces epoxide, hydroxyl, carbonyl and carboxyl groups into graphitic layers.
traps: GO is a family of materials, not a single pure compound
C/O ratio and defect density drive properties
reagents.safety: Oxidized carbon material. Follow material-specific dust and oxidizer history controls.

reagents.name: Nafion dispersion
conditions: Solvent evaporation and annealing affect morphology
Acid form and hydration control conductivity
reagents.scope: Common ionomer for electrochemical devices, catalyst inks and proton-exchange membranes.
reagents.mechanism: Perfluorinated backbone provides stability while sulfonic acid domains conduct protons when hydrated.
traps: Film history and hydration strongly affect performance
Binder loading changes mass transport
reagents.safety: Polymer dispersion. Follow solvent and fluoropolymer handling guidance.

reagents.name: n-Butyllithium
conditions: Dry hydrocarbon or ether solvent
Low-temperature addition for selectivity
reagents.scope: Powerful reagent for generating organolithium species and performing metal-halogen exchange.
reagents.mechanism: Polar organolithium reactivity shaped by aggregation, solvent and substrate acidity.
traps: Titration matters for stoichiometry
Side reactions increase with temperature and electrophile incompatibility
reagents.safety: Pyrophoric solution. Requires trained handling and strict exclusion of air and moisture.

reagents.name: Palladium on carbon
conditions: Hydrogen atmosphere
Solvent and catalyst loading define rate and selectivity
reagents.scope: Heterogeneous palladium catalyst for transfer or gas-phase hydrogenation workflows.
reagents.mechanism: Substrate adsorption, surface hydrogen transfer and product desorption.
traps: Catalyst can ignite when dry
Poisoning and over-reduction must be considered
reagents.safety: Keep wet as supplied and follow hydrogenation risk assessment.

reagents.name: Thionyl chloride
conditions: Often used neat or with catalytic DMF
Gas evolution requires venting strategy
reagents.scope: Converts oxygenated functional groups into more reactive chlorides.
reagents.mechanism: Formation of chlorosulfite or acyl chlorosulfite intermediate followed by substitution.
traps: Stereochemical outcome for alcohols is condition-dependent
Acid-sensitive substrates can degrade
reagents.safety: Corrosive, lachrymatory and releases acidic gases.

reagents.name: Oxalyl chloride
reagents.focus: Acid chloride formation and Swern activation
conditions: Dry solvent
Low temperature for Swern-type oxidation
reagents.scope: Reactive chlorinating and activating agent for acid derivatives and oxidation systems.
reagents.mechanism: Acyl chloride formation or activation of DMSO followed by alkoxysulfonium chemistry.
traps: Gas evolution and exotherm require control
Water-sensitive reagent
reagents.safety: Corrosive and toxic gas evolution. Use strict ventilation and trained handling.

reagents.name: Lawesson's reagent
transformations: Ketone or ester to thiocarbonyl derivative
conditions: Heated organic solvent
Substrate and solvent control selectivity
reagents.scope: Useful sulfur-transfer reagent for thioamide and thiocarbonyl synthesis.
reagents.mechanism: Reactive phosphorus-sulfur species deliver sulfur to carbonyl substrates.
traps: Functional-group compatibility needs procedure evidence
Odor and byproducts complicate work-up
reagents.safety: Use hood controls and consult SDS for sulfur/phosphorus reagent hazards.

reagents.name: 2-Iodoxybenzoic acid
conditions: DMSO or other compatible solvent systems
Temperature and solubility affect rate
reagents.scope: Hypervalent iodine oxidant often compared with Dess-Martin periodinane.
reagents.mechanism: Ligand exchange at iodine followed by elimination to carbonyl product.
traps: Solubility can limit use
Thermal and impact sensitivity reports require caution
reagents.safety: Oxidizer. Avoid heating dry material and follow institutional controls.

reagents.name: Wilkinson's catalyst
conditions: Hydrogen atmosphere
Solvent, ligand and substrate coordination affect activity
reagents.scope: Classic homogeneous hydrogenation catalyst with strong mechanistic teaching value.
reagents.mechanism: Ligand dissociation, oxidative addition of hydrogen, alkene coordination, migratory insertion and reductive elimination.
traps: Poisoning by strongly coordinating groups
Air sensitivity varies with handling and purity
reagents.safety: Precious-metal catalyst and hydrogenation conditions require suitable controls.

reagents.name: Swern oxidation system
conditions: Low temperature
Dry solvent
Base quench after activation
reagents.scope: Useful when chromium oxidants are undesirable and acid-sensitive substrates are tolerated.
reagents.mechanism: Activated DMSO forms an alkoxysulfonium intermediate followed by base-promoted elimination.
traps: Temperature control is important
Dimethyl sulfide odor and gas evolution require planning
reagents.safety: Corrosive activator and malodorous byproducts. Use a hood and trained procedure.

reagents.name: TPAP/NMO
conditions: Catalytic TPAP with NMO co-oxidant
Molecular sieves often used
reagents.scope: Mild catalytic oxidation system for alcohols where substrate compatibility is favorable.
reagents.mechanism: Ruthenium oxo species oxidizes alcohol and is regenerated by NMO.
traps: Water and substrate coordination affect outcome
Procedure-specific loading matters
reagents.safety: Oxidizing system with heavy-metal catalyst. Follow waste controls.

reagents.name: DAST
transformations: Carbonyl to gem-difluoride in selected cases
conditions: Dry solvent
Low-temperature addition frequently used
reagents.scope: Common deoxyfluorinating reagent for introducing C-F bonds under controlled conditions.
reagents.mechanism: Sulfur-fluoride activation followed by substitution or fluorination pathway.
traps: Elimination and rearrangement can compete
Thermal instability requires caution
reagents.safety: Reactive fluorinating reagent. Avoid heating and incompatible nucleophiles.

reagents.name: Carbonyldiimidazole
conditions: Aprotic solvent
Addition of nucleophile after activation
reagents.scope: Useful activating reagent where imidazole byproduct is operationally convenient.
reagents.mechanism: Nucleophilic acyl substitution at activated carbonyl intermediate.
traps: Moisture consumes reagent
Hindered acids or amines may need optimization
reagents.safety: Moisture-reactive irritant. Use standard dry handling.

reagents.name: PyBOP
transformations: Carboxylic acid plus amine to amide
conditions: Base such as DIPEA
Polar aprotic solvent
reagents.scope: Phosphonium coupling reagent used for amide-bond formation with reduced racemization in many peptide contexts.
reagents.mechanism: Carboxylate activation followed by amine attack and phosphonium byproduct formation.
traps: Not automatically racemization-free
Base and additive choice matters
reagents.safety: Consult SDS and peptide-coupling waste procedures.

reagents.name: 1-Hydroxybenzotriazole
transformations: Racemization suppression in selected peptide couplings
conditions: Used with carbodiimide or phosphonium coupling systems
reagents.scope: Additive for amide coupling that changes leaving-group and side-reaction profile.
reagents.mechanism: Forms benzotriazolyl ester intermediate with improved aminolysis behavior.
traps: Hydration state and handling form matter
Dry material may be energetic
reagents.safety: Potential energetic hazard when dry. Follow supplier and institutional controls.

reagents.name: Potassium bis(trimethylsilyl)amide
conditions: Dry THF or hydrocarbon solvent
Temperature controls aggregation and selectivity
reagents.scope: Strong non-nucleophilic base with cation effects distinct from lithium analogues.
reagents.mechanism: Sterically hindered amide abstracts acidic proton; counterion changes aggregation and reactivity.
traps: Cation effects can change E/Z enolate ratio
Moisture destroys reagent
reagents.safety: Strong base, moisture-sensitive solution or solid.

reagents.name: Sodium azide
reagents.focus: Azide substitution and click precursor synthesis
transformations: Acyl derivative to acyl azide in selected workflows
conditions: Polar aprotic solvent often supports substitution
Avoid acid and heavy metals
reagents.scope: Useful azide source for substitution and downstream triazole or amine routes.
reagents.mechanism: Azide anion acts as nucleophile in substitution or acyl transfer chemistry.
traps: Hydrazoic acid formation is dangerous
Heavy-metal azides can be explosive
reagents.safety: Highly toxic and potentially explosive with incompatible metals/acids.

reagents.name: AD-mix-alpha
conditions: Biphasic tert-butanol/water often used
Ligand set controls facial selectivity
reagents.scope: Packaged asymmetric dihydroxylation system for many alkenes.
reagents.mechanism: Osmium-catalyzed syn dihydroxylation with chiral ligand-controlled approach.
traps: Mnemonic selectivity rules need substrate-specific confirmation
Osmium toxicity dominates risk
reagents.safety: Contains toxic osmium species. Follow specialized waste controls.

reagents.name: Triphosgene
transformations: Amine to isocyanate or carbamate in selected workflows
conditions: Base and dry solvent
Controlled addition and ventilation
reagents.scope: Solid phosgene equivalent used for carbonyl-transfer chemistry.
reagents.mechanism: Thermal or nucleophile-triggered generation of reactive carbonyl chloride equivalents.
traps: Can release phosgene
Stoichiometry and base control product class
reagents.safety: Highly toxic phosgene-equivalent. Requires strict controls.

reagents.name: Methanol
reagents.safety: PubChem GHS safety summary added for search display.
hazardStatements: H225: Highly Flammable liquid and vapor [Danger Flammable liquids]
H301: Toxic if swallowed [Danger Acute toxicity, oral]
H311: Toxic in contact with skin [Danger Acute toxicity, dermal]
H331: Toxic if inhaled [Danger Acute toxicity, inhalation]
H370 **: Causes damage to organs [Danger Specific target organ toxicity, single exposure]
reagents.disposalMethod: Collect as hazardous toxic flammable organic-waste; keep segregated and route through institutional EHS.

reagents.name: Ethanol
reagents.safety: PubChem GHS safety summary added for search display.
hazardStatements: H225: Highly Flammable liquid and vapor [Danger Flammable liquids]
reagents.disposalMethod: Collect in a compatible flammable organic-waste container with ignition sources excluded; do not pour to drain.

mechanisms.name: SN1 substitution
mechanisms.summary: Two-step substitution through a carbocation intermediate.
mechanisms.rateLaw: rate = k[substrate]
mechanisms.stereo: Racemisation or partial inversion/retention depending on ion-pair effects.
steps: Leaving group departs to form a carbocation
Deprotonation gives the neutral substitution product
traps: Rearrangements are possible
Primary substrates usually do not favour SN1

mechanisms.name: SN2 substitution
mechanisms.summary: One-step backside attack with simultaneous leaving-group departure.
mechanisms.rateLaw: rate = k[substrate][nucleophile]
mechanisms.stereo: Inversion of configuration at the reacting carbon.
steps: Leaving group departs as the new bond forms
traps: Steric hindrance slows SN2 sharply
Tertiary substrates are poor SN2 candidates

mechanisms.name: E2 elimination
mechanisms.summary: Concerted base-induced elimination that forms an alkene.
mechanisms.rateLaw: rate = k[substrate][base]
mechanisms.stereo: Requires an anti-periplanar arrangement in many systems.
steps: C-H electrons form the pi bond
Leaving group exits in the same step
traps: Bulky bases can favour Hofmann products
Stereochemical geometry can control which alkene forms

mechanisms.name: Nucleophilic addition to carbonyls
mechanisms.summary: Nucleophile attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon.
mechanisms.rateLaw: Depends on nucleophile and catalysis.
mechanisms.stereo: Planar carbonyls can form new stereocentres after attack.
steps: Pi bond opens to form an alkoxide
Protonation gives alcohol or further chemistry follows
traps: Aldehydes are usually more reactive than ketones
Acid/base conditions change the activated species

mechanisms.name: Electrophilic aromatic substitution
mechanisms.summary: Aromatic ring substitutes H for an electrophile while restoring aromaticity.
mechanisms.rateLaw: Often electrophile generation and ring activation dominate.
mechanisms.stereo: Regiochemistry is controlled by substituent directing effects.
steps: Aromatic pi electrons attack to form sigma complex
Base removes H+ and aromaticity returns
traps: Activating groups speed reaction
Deactivating groups can direct meta

mechanisms.name: Grignard carbonyl addition
mechanisms.summary: Organomagnesium reagent adds carbon to a carbonyl, then work-up gives alcohol.
mechanisms.rateLaw: Operationally controlled by organometallic addition and work-up.
mechanisms.stereo: Planar carbonyl attack can create stereocentres.
traps: Water destroys the reagent
Product class depends on carbonyl starting material

mechanisms.name: Allylic radical bromination
mechanisms.summary: Selective bromination through resonance-stabilised allylic radical intermediates.
mechanisms.rateLaw: Chain reactions depend on initiation, propagation and termination steps.
mechanisms.stereo: Allylic radical geometry and resonance can create product mixtures.
steps: Allylic hydrogen abstraction forms resonance-stabilised radical
Bromine transfer gives allylic bromide and continues chain
traps: Bromination at the double bond is a different ionic pathway
Multiple allylic sites reduce selectivity

mechanisms.name: Enolate alkylation
mechanisms.summary: Alpha deprotonation creates an enolate that forms a new C-C bond with an electrophile.
mechanisms.rateLaw: Operationally controlled by deprotonation, aggregation and electrophile trapping.
mechanisms.stereo: Enolate geometry and facial selectivity can control stereochemical outcome.
steps: Enolate forms with oxygen and carbon resonance contributors
Carbon attack on electrophile forms alpha-substituted product
traps: O-alkylation versus C-alkylation can be context-dependent
Kinetic and thermodynamic enolates are not interchangeable

mechanisms.name: Wittig olefination
mechanisms.summary: Phosphorus ylide converts aldehydes or ketones into alkenes.
mechanisms.rateLaw: Depends on ylide class, carbonyl electrophilicity and solvent conditions.
mechanisms.stereo: Ylide stabilisation and conditions influence E/Z alkene ratio.
steps: Collapse gives alkene and phosphine oxide
traps: Do not lose track of which ylide carbon becomes part of the alkene
Stereoselectivity is not universal

mechanisms.name: Baeyer-Villiger oxidation
mechanisms.summary: Peracid inserts oxygen next to a carbonyl to form an ester or lactone.
mechanisms.rateLaw: Depends on carbonyl activation, peroxide reagent and rearrangement step.
mechanisms.stereo: Migrating group retains its configuration in standard mechanistic descriptions.
steps: Group migration occurs as leaving group departs
traps: Migratory aptitude determines regiochemistry
This is not simple alkene epoxidation even though peracids can do both

mechanisms.name: Oxidative addition
mechanisms.summary: A low-valent metal inserts into a sigma bond, commonly raising metal oxidation state and coordination number in cross-coupling cycles.
mechanisms.rateLaw: Often catalyst and aryl halide dependent; ligand electronics and halide identity strongly alter apparent rate.
mechanisms.stereo: Stereochemistry is substrate-specific; oxidative addition to vinyl electrophiles can preserve or alter geometry depending on pathway.
steps: C-X bond activation forms organometal halide
Ligand set controls access to later transmetalation or insertion steps
traps: Aryl chloride activation may be slow
Catalyst resting state can be off-cycle
Ligand oxidation state language must match evidence

mechanisms.name: Transmetalation gate
mechanisms.summary: Organic group transfer from boron, tin, zinc or copper to a transition metal joins two fragments before reductive elimination.
mechanisms.rateLaw: Base, water and counter-ion terms can dominate apparent kinetics.
mechanisms.stereo: Usually retains organic fragment connectivity; stereochemical claims require product analysis.
steps: Carbon fragment transfer to metal center
traps: Protodeboronation competes
Counter-ion effects are often hidden
Water can accelerate or destroy the partner

mechanisms.name: Reductive elimination
mechanisms.summary: Two ligands on a metal form a new covalent bond while the metal returns to a lower oxidation state.
mechanisms.rateLaw: Often depends on ligand bite angle, sterics and concentration of active catalytic species.
mechanisms.stereo: Cis ligand arrangement is commonly required; stereochemical transfer depends on ligand geometry.
steps: Bond formation occurs from metal coordination sphere
traps: Trans ligands may not eliminate
Product inhibition can slow turnover
Metal black suggests catalyst death but not mechanism proof

mechanisms.name: Migratory insertion
mechanisms.summary: An unsaturated ligand inserts into a metal-carbon or metal-hydride bond, creating a new organometallic intermediate.
mechanisms.rateLaw: Substrate coordination and ligand dissociation frequently appear in kinetic models.
mechanisms.stereo: Syn insertion geometry can govern alkene stereochemistry and regiochemistry.
steps: Alkene or carbonyl coordinates to metal
New metal-carbon or metal-acyl intermediate forms
traps: Regioisomer mixtures
beta-hydride elimination follows rapidly
Coordination sites may be blocked

mechanisms.name: Beta-hydride elimination
mechanisms.summary: A beta hydrogen transfers to a metal center, forming an alkene and a metal hydride species.
mechanisms.rateLaw: Requires accessible beta C-H bond and suitable metal coordination geometry.
mechanisms.stereo: Syn-coplanar requirements can control alkene geometry.
traps: Unwanted isomerization
chain walking
confusing elimination with direct substitution

mechanisms.name: Nucleophilic acyl substitution
mechanisms.summary: A nucleophile adds to an acyl derivative and the tetrahedral intermediate collapses to expel a leaving group.
mechanisms.rateLaw: Depends on nucleophile strength, leaving-group ability and acid-base conditions.
mechanisms.stereo: Chiral acyl partners can racemize if activation generates enolizable or oxazolone-like intermediates.
steps: Acyl activation or direct nucleophilic attack
Leaving group expulsion and proton transfer
traps: Hydrolysis competes
overacylation
racemization of amino acid derivatives

mechanisms.name: Activation and leaving-group engineering
mechanisms.summary: A poor leaving group is converted into a better one so substitution, elimination or coupling becomes kinetically feasible.
mechanisms.rateLaw: Often activation reagent and substrate concentration dependent, followed by nucleophile-dependent capture.
mechanisms.stereo: SN2 capture inverts stereocenters, while cationic pathways can scramble stereochemistry.
steps: Nucleophile captures or leaving group departs
traps: Activated intermediate hydrolysis
neighboring-group participation
overactivation

mechanisms.name: Imine and iminium ion chemistry
mechanisms.summary: Amine and carbonyl condensation creates imine or iminium intermediates that can be reduced or trapped by nucleophiles.
mechanisms.rateLaw: pH has a bell-shaped effect because amine nucleophilicity and carbonyl activation compete.
mechanisms.stereo: Chiral amines, hydride face selectivity and substrate conformation can control stereochemical outcome.
traps: Water suppresses imine formation
overalkylation
direct carbonyl reduction

mechanisms.name: Hydride transfer
mechanisms.summary: A formal hydride equivalent is delivered to an electrophilic center, often carbonyl carbon or iminium carbon.
mechanisms.rateLaw: Electrophile identity, solvent and Lewis acid activation determine apparent rate.
mechanisms.stereo: Diastereofacial selectivity follows substrate conformation, chelation and reagent architecture.
traps: functional-group overreduction
quench hazards
borate or aluminum salt work-up artifacts

mechanisms.name: Pericyclic cycloaddition
mechanisms.summary: Bond reorganization occurs through a cyclic transition state where orbital symmetry controls feasibility and stereochemical transfer.
mechanisms.rateLaw: Second-order substrate terms are common for bimolecular cycloadditions; Lewis acids can change rate and selectivity.
mechanisms.stereo: Stereospecific; relative alkene geometry is often retained in the product framework.
steps: sigma bonds form without discrete ions
traps: Stepwise radical or ionic alternatives
thermal reversibility
incorrect endo/exo assignment

mechanisms.name: Sol-gel hydrolysis and condensation
mechanisms.summary: Metal or silicon alkoxides hydrolyze and condense into M-O-M networks whose porosity and morphology reflect water, catalyst and aging history.
mechanisms.rateLaw: Hydrolysis and condensation have separate pH and water-ratio dependencies.
mechanisms.stereo: Not a molecular stereochemical mechanism; network topology and particle morphology replace molecule-level stereochemistry.
traps: uncontrolled precipitation
drying cracks
confusing gel formation with crystallinity

mechanisms.name: Surface silanization
mechanisms.summary: Hydrolyzed silanes condense with hydroxylated surfaces or with each other, creating functional organic-inorganic interfaces.
mechanisms.rateLaw: Surface hydroxyl density, humidity and silane concentration control apparent coverage.
mechanisms.stereo: Surface orientation and multilayer formation matter more than molecular stereochemistry.
steps: curing and removal of physisorbed silane
traps: oligomeric silane deposits
insufficient washing
overstated monolayer claims

mechanisms.name: ATRP activation-deactivation equilibrium
mechanisms.summary: Dormant alkyl halide chains reversibly form radicals under copper catalysis, limiting radical concentration and controlling chain growth.
mechanisms.rateLaw: Activation, deactivation and propagation terms combine; oxygen removes active Cu(I) catalyst.
mechanisms.stereo: Tacticity is usually not controlled unless monomer and catalyst design impose stereochemical bias.
steps: Cu(II) deactivates radical back to dormant chain
traps: oxygen inhibition
loss of end-group fidelity
copper contamination

mechanisms.name: RAFT addition-fragmentation
mechanisms.summary: A thiocarbonylthio chain-transfer agent mediates radical exchange so growing chains experience similar growth histories.
mechanisms.rateLaw: Propagation, addition to CTA and fragmentation rates must be balanced for narrow dispersity.
mechanisms.stereo: RAFT controls molar mass distribution more than stereochemistry.
traps: wrong CTA for monomer class
colored end groups
retardation

mechanisms.name: Radical chain propagation
mechanisms.summary: Radical intermediates consume substrates and regenerate radicals through propagation steps that can outpace initiation.
mechanisms.rateLaw: May show light, initiator or inhibitor dependence; quantum yield above unity suggests chain character.
mechanisms.stereo: Often less stereospecific than concerted pathways unless radical clocks or cages constrain geometry.
traps: oxygen inhibition
radical scavenger ambiguity
mistaking initiation for turnover-limiting step

mechanisms.name: Photoredox quenching cycle
mechanisms.summary: An excited photocatalyst transfers an electron or energy to a quencher, generating reactive open-shell intermediates under visible light.
mechanisms.rateLaw: Stern-Volmer quenching, light intensity and catalyst loading can define the kinetic boundary.
mechanisms.stereo: Stereochemical control is usually supplied by a second catalyst, substrate geometry or radical-pair environment.
steps: back electron transfer or radical propagation
traps: unmeasured light flux
photobleaching
thermal background reaction

mechanisms.name: Meisenheimer SNAr pathway
mechanisms.summary: A nucleophile adds to an electron-poor aryl halide to form a sigma-adduct that eliminates leaving group and restores aromaticity.
mechanisms.rateLaw: Nucleophile and aryl electrophile terms are expected when addition is rate limiting.
mechanisms.stereo: No stereocenter is usually created at the aryl carbon; regioselectivity follows activating group placement.
traps: benzyne alternative under harsh base
unactivated aryl chloride failure
incorrect leaving-group trend assumptions

mechanisms.name: Diazonium substitution manifold
mechanisms.summary: Aryl diazonium salts can release nitrogen and enter ionic, radical or metal-mediated substitution and coupling pathways.
mechanisms.rateLaw: Often sensitive to temperature, copper salt, counter-ion and trapping nucleophile concentration.
mechanisms.stereo: Aryl substitution does not normally report stereochemistry, but regioselectivity in azo coupling is pH and ring-activation dependent.
steps: capture by nucleophile, arene or surface
traps: unstable dry salts
side reduction
overcoupling in azo dye formation

mechanisms.name: Ring-opening polymerization mechanism
mechanisms.summary: A cyclic monomer opens at a reactive chain end, converting ring strain or heteroatom activation into polymer growth.
mechanisms.rateLaw: Monomer, initiator and catalyst terms depend on coordination-insertion, anionic, cationic or organocatalytic pathway.
mechanisms.stereo: Monomer stereochemistry and catalyst environment can control tacticity and crystallinity.
steps: chain propagation and termination or quench
traps: transesterification
adventitious water initiation
broad dispersity at high conversion

mechanisms.name: Electrochemical electron transfer
mechanisms.summary: An electrode supplies or removes electrons at a defined interface, generating intermediates without stoichiometric redox reagents.
mechanisms.rateLaw: Current, potential, mass transport and electrode area jointly shape observed rate.
mechanisms.stereo: Stereochemical outcome depends on follow-up chemistry after electron transfer.
traps: unreported electrode area
passivation
supporting electrolyte participation

mechanisms.name: Michaelis-Menten enzyme turnover
mechanisms.summary: An enzyme binds substrate, forms a catalytic complex and releases product with saturation behavior at high substrate concentration.
mechanisms.rateLaw: v = Vmax[S]/(Km + [S]) under initial-rate assumptions.
mechanisms.stereo: Chiral active sites can deliver high enantioselectivity or diastereoselectivity.
traps: substrate inhibition
mass-transfer artifacts
cofactor depletion

mechanisms.name: Acylium ion electrophilic aromatic substitution
mechanisms.summary: A Lewis acid activates an acid chloride or anhydride to generate an acylium-like electrophile that substitutes onto an aromatic ring.
mechanisms.rateLaw: Arene electronics, electrophile generation and Lewis acid stoichiometry govern observed rate.
mechanisms.stereo: Regiochemistry follows substituent directing effects; stereochemistry is usually not central.
traps: Lewis acid complexation
deactivated arenes
hydrolysis of acid chloride

spectra.assignment: O-H stretch in alcohols or phenols
spectra.clue: Broad due to hydrogen bonding.
Often appears with a C=O band near 1710-1725 cm-1.
Exact position helps separate acid, ester, aldehyde, ketone and amide.
Nitriles are often sharper and stronger than alkynes.
Triplet and quartet often suggest an ethyl group.

spectra.assignment: Allylic, benzylic or alpha to carbonyl
spectra.clue: Look for nearby pi systems or carbonyl groups.

spectra.assignment: Protons on carbon attached to O, N or halogen
spectra.clue: Often indicates alcohols, ethers, amines or halogenoalkanes.
A monosubstituted benzene often integrates near 5H.
Usually distinctive and downfield.
More substituted carbons can appear further downfield.
Alcohol and ether carbons commonly appear here.
Aromatic rings can generate several signals depending on symmetry.

spectra.assignment: Acid, ester, amide or acyl derivative carbonyl
spectra.clue: Often less downfield than aldehyde and ketone carbonyls.
Strong evidence for aldehydes or ketones when paired with IR C=O.
Bromine is close to 1:1; chlorine is close to 3:1.
Often weaker than carbonyl bands; combine with 1H NMR alkene region.
Pair with C triple bond C region near 2100-2260 cm-1.
Exchangeable and concentration-dependent; do not rely on it alone.
Coupling constants can support alkene geometry.

spectra.assignment: Acetal, ketal or anomeric carbon region
spectra.clue: High-value clue for carbohydrate and protected carbonyl contexts.

spectra.signal: extended conjugation shifts lambda max longer
spectra.clue: Use with calibration and solvent context before making quantitative claims.

elements.name: Boron
elements.notes: Appears in borohydride reagents and Lewis acid chemistry.

elements.notes: Core framework element for organic chemistry.

elements.name: Oxygen
elements.notes: Alcohols, ethers, carbonyls, acids and hydrogen bonding.

elements.name: Aluminium

elements.name: Phosphorus

elements.name: Sulfur

elements.name: Chlorine

elements.name: Potassium

elements.name: Manganese
