quiz.question: Which reagent is usually suitable for reducing a ketone to a secondary alcohol?
quiz.note: NaBH4 is a mild hydride donor for aldehydes and ketones.

quiz.question: Which technique commonly shows a broad alcohol O-H stretch?
quiz.note: A broad O-H stretch often appears around 3200-3600 cm-1.

quiz.question: PCC is commonly chosen over acidified dichromate when the target from a primary alcohol is what?
quiz.note: PCC can stop at aldehyde under anhydrous conditions.

quiz.question: Which condition is strongly associated with Grignard reactions before work-up?
quiz.note: Grignard reagents are destroyed by proton sources.

quiz.question: Which mechanism has a rate law that depends on both substrate and nucleophile?
quiz.note: SN2 is bimolecular in the rate-determining transition state.

quiz.question: A 1:1 M and M+2 mass spectrum pattern is a strong clue for which element?
quiz.note: Bromine isotopes commonly create an approximately equal M and M+2 pair.

quiz.question: Cold dilute alkaline KMnO4 with an alkene most directly suggests which product class?
quiz.note: Hot concentrated conditions are associated with oxidative cleavage.

quiz.question: Which mechanism requires backside attack and gives inversion at the reacting carbon?
quiz.note: SN2 substitution proceeds by backside attack.

routes.note: A hydration route may be simpler if the syllabus includes acid-catalysed alkene hydration.
LiAlH4 also works but is stronger than needed in many teaching contexts.
The carbon group added by RMgX becomes a new substituent on the alcohol carbon.
Avoid refluxing acidified dichromate if aldehyde is the target.

route: Strong oxidation with acidified dichromate or suitable equivalent
routes.note: This is a contrast case for selective oxidation.
The catalyst is needed because benzene is relatively stable.
Acylation avoids some rearrangement problems associated with alkylation.
Moisture control matters because acyl chlorides hydrolyse readily.
Peracid epoxidation preserves useful stereochemical relationships in many alkene cases.
The work-up controls whether aldehydes are retained or further oxidized.
Ylide identity and conditions affect alkene geometry.
Migratory aptitude controls which group moves next to oxygen.
Kinetic and thermodynamic enolate claims require condition context.
Tosylation activates an alcohol while retaining configuration at the carbon center.
Primary and secondary alcohols often follow substitution logic distinct from carbocation routes.
Coupling reagent choice affects side reactions, solubility and purification burden.
Catalyst poisoning is the basis for stopping before alkane formation.
This route contrasts with Lindlar syn hydrogenation.
Protecting group choice controls stability and later deprotection.
Substrate sensitivity and silyl group identity control deprotection rate.
Substituent electronics determine the regiochemical prediction.
Boc protection changes basicity and acid sensitivity.
Other acid-labile groups must be checked before using TFA.
Polymerization claims need conversion, molecular weight and inhibitor history.
Optical and mechanical claims depend on molecular weight and residual monomer.
Moisture exclusion and substrate sensitivity determine practical success.
Thionation should be supported by diagnostic spectroscopy or reference comparison.
Suzuki logic requires halide identity, boron partner stability, base and catalyst-ligand evidence.
Buchwald-Hartwig claims should track monoarylation, base compatibility and ligand choice.
Audit Glaser homocoupling and copper residue.
Reductive amination needs pH, water and overalkylation control.
Sharpless epoxidation requires ee measurement and substrate geometry context.
Anti versus syn oxygenation should be justified with mechanism and product stereochemistry.
Water ratio, pH and drying history determine porosity and cracking.
PXRD alone is insufficient; porosity and defect evidence are needed.
Report conversion, Mn, dispersity and end-group fidelity.
NCO index, moisture and cure schedule explain network properties.
Green chemistry claims need mass balance, catalyst lifetime and solvent boundary.

evidenceStandards.name: Reference data
evidenceStandards.standard: Prefer evaluated databases and record units, phase, solvent and method.

evidenceStandards.name: Checked synthesis
evidenceStandards.standard: Prefer independently checked or peer-reviewed procedures with characterization.

evidenceStandards.name: Terminology
evidenceStandards.standard: Use IUPAC terminology when a term has a formal definition.

evidenceStandards.name: Teaching heuristic
evidenceStandards.claim: Exam trap, rule of thumb or revision pattern
evidenceStandards.standard: Label as heuristic and connect it to a mechanistic reason.

sources.title: IUPAC Compendium of Chemical Terminology
sources.note: Controlled terminology for definitions such as electrophile, aromaticity and Beer-Lambert law.

sources.title: IUPAC Gold Book: Beer-Lambert law
sources.note: Formal terminology entry for absorbance-based concentration relationships.

sources.title: IUPAC Gold Book: electrophile
sources.note: Terminology anchor for electron-pair accepting reagents.

sources.title: NIST Chemistry WebBook, SRD 69
sources.note: Evaluated thermochemical, spectral, ion energetics and compound data.

sources.title: Organic Syntheses
sources.note: Peer-reviewed, checked experimental procedures and characterization data.

sources.title: PubChem
sources.note: Open compound identifiers, structures, properties and cross-linked records.

sources.title: ACS Green Chemistry Principles
sources.note: Design principles for reducing hazard, waste and environmental burden.

concepts.definition: A reagent or site that accepts an electron pair from a nucleophile during bond formation.
concepts.academicUse: Use the term to explain why carbonyl carbons, activated halogens and acylium ions can be attacked by electron-rich species.
concepts.evidenceNote: A terminology-level claim should point to IUPAC; a substrate-specific claim needs experimental or computational support.

concepts.definition: An electron-pair donor that forms a bond to an electrophilic atom or centre.
concepts.academicUse: Compare nucleophilicity separately from basicity; solvent, polarizability and sterics can change ranking.
concepts.evidenceNote: Do not treat a simple nucleophile table as universal without specifying solvent, substrate and mechanism.

concepts.definition: A stabilising electronic property associated with cyclic conjugated systems; simple Huckel counting is a useful entry model.
concepts.academicUse: Use it to justify the substitution pattern of benzene chemistry and the energetic penalty for addition reactions.
concepts.evidenceNote: For advanced work, magnetic, energetic and structural criteria can disagree; label which criterion is being used.

concepts.definition: A proportional relationship between absorbance, path length and concentration under appropriate optical conditions.
concepts.academicUse: Use it to estimate concentration from UV-visible absorbance after calibration and blank correction.
concepts.evidenceNote: Report wavelength, solvent, path length, linear range and calibration quality.

concepts.definition: A mathematical statement connecting reaction rate to reactant concentrations and experimentally determined orders.
concepts.academicUse: Use kinetic order as evidence for or against a proposed rate-determining step.
concepts.evidenceNote: Mechanism cannot be proven by a rate law alone; it constrains plausible mechanisms.

concepts.definition: A relative NMR resonance position that reflects magnetic shielding and local electronic environment.
concepts.academicUse: Combine shift, integration, multiplicity and coupling before assigning a proton environment.
concepts.evidenceNote: Assignments are strongest when NMR, IR, MS and known synthetic context agree.

concepts.definition: Carbonyl reactions often begin from polarisation of the C=O bond, making carbon susceptible to nucleophilic attack.
concepts.academicUse: Explain trends across aldehydes, ketones, acyl chlorides, esters, amides and carboxylic acids.
concepts.evidenceNote: Separate electronic activation, sterics and leaving-group ability rather than using one generic reactivity scale.

concepts.definition: Quantitative and qualitative tools for comparing waste, hazard, efficiency and environmental impact.
concepts.academicUse: Use alongside yield; a high-yield reaction can still be poor if it uses hazardous solvents or generates large waste.
concepts.evidenceNote: Report boundaries: solvent included or excluded, purification burden and scale.

concepts.equation: functional group A reacts preferentially over B
concepts.definition: Preferential reaction of one functional group or reactive site in the presence of another.
concepts.academicUse: Use it to justify reagent choice in substrates containing multiple reducible, oxidizable or nucleophilic sites.
concepts.evidenceNote: A chemoselectivity claim needs substrate, solvent, temperature and competing functionality context.

concepts.definition: Preference for bond formation or cleavage at one position over other possible positions.
concepts.academicUse: Use it for Markovnikov addition, aromatic directing effects, allylic substitution and Baeyer-Villiger migration patterns.
concepts.evidenceNote: Report product ratio or state that the claim is only a teaching prediction.

concepts.definition: The capacity for an observation or result to be obtained again under stated conditions and assumptions.
concepts.academicUse: Use it to separate a polished explanation from a research-ready record with raw data and metadata.
concepts.evidenceNote: A reproducibility claim should name missing metadata, raw data availability and procedure source.

concepts.definition: The bounded doubt attached to a measurement, assignment or calculated quantity.
concepts.academicUse: Use it to avoid overstating spectral assignments, concentration calculations and route comparisons.
concepts.evidenceNote: State whether uncertainty is instrumental, procedural, interpretive or statistical.

concepts.definition: Temporary functional-group masking used to control chemoselectivity during a sequence.
concepts.academicUse: Use it to justify why a functional group is intentionally modified before the target transformation.
concepts.evidenceNote: A protecting-group argument should state installation conditions, stability window and deprotection plan.

concepts.definition: A common conceptual cycle for palladium-catalyzed formation of C-C and C-heteroatom bonds.
concepts.academicUse: Use it to explain how electrophile identity, ligand, base and organometallic partner shape coupling outcomes.
concepts.evidenceNote: Catalyst claims require ligand, oxidation state, base, solvent and substrate class context.

concepts.definition: A family of polymerization methods that regulate active radical concentration to improve molecular-weight control.
concepts.academicUse: Use it for ATRP and related polymer synthesis discussions where dispersity and chain-end fidelity matter.
concepts.evidenceNote: Report initiator ratio, conversion, dispersity and oxygen-control assumptions.

concepts.equation: M-OR hydrolysis + M-OH condensation -> M-O-M network
concepts.definition: A wet-chemical route where molecular precursors form oxide or hybrid networks through hydrolysis and condensation.
concepts.academicUse: Use it to connect precursor chemistry with particle size, porosity, film formation and calcination history.
concepts.evidenceNote: pH, water ratio, aging, drying and heat treatment are structural variables and must be recorded.

concepts.definition: Chemical modification of a material surface to alter wetting, charge, reactivity, adhesion or bioconjugation.
concepts.academicUse: Use it to explain how EDC coupling, silanization, ionomer films or carbon oxidation change material interfaces.
concepts.evidenceNote: Surface claims need characterization such as spectroscopy, contact angle, zeta potential or electrochemical response.

concepts.equation: name + identifier + structure + source link -> citable identity
concepts.definition: A local chemistry record that distinguishes human-readable names from machine identifiers such as CID, InChIKey, SMILES and CAS number.
concepts.academicUse: Use it before comparing literature, spectra or safety data for a compound with multiple synonyms.
concepts.evidenceNote: At least one public identifier and a source link should be retained for reproducibility.

concepts.equation: local miss -> external query -> structured source card
concepts.definition: A workflow where a static research site requests public scholarly metadata and renders source-linked cards without copying restricted full text.
concepts.academicUse: Use it to keep local notes concise while preserving traceability to NIH/NLM and PubChem records.
concepts.evidenceNote: Imported metadata is a discovery layer, not a substitute for reading the original source.

concepts.equation: substrates + catalyst + conditions + readouts + failure modes -> defensible reaction claim
concepts.definition: A structured record that treats a reaction as an evidence-bearing system rather than a single named transformation.
concepts.academicUse: Use it to compare methods, screen conditions and avoid unsupported mechanism claims.
concepts.evidenceNote: A complete record should include negative outcomes and impurity hypotheses, not just isolated yield.

concepts.equation: kinetics + isotope effects + stereochemistry + intermediates + controls -> mechanism argument
concepts.definition: A layered way to describe how multiple measurements constrain plausible reaction mechanisms.
concepts.academicUse: Use it when a named mechanism is tempting but insufficiently proven by product identity alone.
concepts.evidenceNote: No single measurement normally proves a mechanism; it narrows alternatives.

concepts.equation: synthesis history + structure + morphology + property test -> material claim
concepts.definition: A guardrail for stating material performance only within the characterization and processing history actually measured.
concepts.academicUse: Use it to prevent overgeneralizing from one batch, morphology or test condition.
concepts.evidenceNote: Performance claims should normalize geometry, loading, active mass and environmental conditions.

compounds.name: Bromobenzene

compounds.evidenceNote: Conversion claims should distinguish coupling, dehalogenation and homocoupling products.

compounds.name: Iodobenzene
compounds.summary: Highly reactive aryl halide partner in oxidative addition and hypervalent iodine chemistry contexts.
compounds.evidenceNote: Iodoarene impurities and photochemical history can affect reactions.

compounds.name: Chlorobenzene
compounds.summary: Less reactive aryl chloride model for catalyst and ligand strength in cross-coupling.
compounds.evidenceNote: Successful C-Cl activation requires explicit catalyst/ligand context.

compounds.name: Maleic anhydride

compounds.evidenceNote: Hydrolysis state and isomer purity affect interpretation.

compounds.name: Furfural

compounds.evidenceNote: Feedstock origin and inhibitor content should be recorded for catalytic studies.

compounds.name: Levulinic acid

compounds.evidenceNote: Water content and acid impurities matter in esterification or hydrogenation reports.

compounds.name: Lactic acid

compounds.evidenceNote: Report enantiomeric composition and water content.

compounds.name: Caprolactam

reactants.name: Aryl halide electrophile
evidence: NMR assignment of biaryl or aryl amine product
constraints: Aryl chlorides usually require more active catalysts

reactants.name: Aryl or vinyl boronic acid
constraints: Protodeboronation can dominate electron-poor heteroaryl systems
Water and base change transmetalation rate

reactants.name: Terminal alkyne
evidence: IR C-H stretch near terminal alkyne region
constraints: Copper and oxygen can generate diyne byproducts
Strong base can affect sensitive groups

reactants.name: Aldehyde or ketone
evidence: Chemoselectivity against ester or amide groups
constraints: Aldehydes are more reactive than ketones
Enolizable carbonyls need base and temperature control

reactants.name: Carboxylic acid platform
evidence: Residual acid titration or LC trace
constraints: Base and coupling reagent choice changes byproduct profile

reactants.name: Amine nucleophile
constraints: Anilines are weaker nucleophiles than alkyl amines
Steric and basicity effects control conversion

reactants.name: Alcohol or polyol
constraints: Primary and secondary alcohols diverge under oxidation
Phenols are more acidic and less SN2-like

reactants.name: Epoxide
constraints: Acidic and basic conditions invert regioselectivity trends
Water and amines can compete strongly

reactants.name: Diene and dienophile pair
constraints: Temperature can change kinetic versus thermodynamic product ratios

reactants.name: Organic azide and alkyne pair
evidence: Copper residue assessment for biological use
constraints: Copper compatibility matters in biomolecular systems
Strain-promoted variants avoid copper but alter kinetics

reactants.name: Isocyanate and polyol
constraints: Water generates CO2 and urea linkages
Catalyst changes pot life and network topology

reactants.name: Metal salt precursor
constraints: pH and ligand field control nucleation
Residual anions can change material performance

reactants.name: Silane coupling agent
evidence: Contact angle or XPS surface change
constraints: Moisture is required but excess water causes oligomerization

reactants.name: Biomass oxygenate
constraints: Mixtures and water content complicate kinetics
Humins or char can mask catalyst deactivation

reactants.name: Nitrile platform
constraints: Hydrolysis can stop at amide or continue to acid

reactants.name: Aryl diazonium salt
constraints: water and pH alter coupling outcome

reactionSystems.name: Suzuki-Miyaura cross-coupling
conditions: Pd catalyst and ligand system
carbonate, phosphate or hydroxide base
aqueous organic solvent frequently used
nextQuestions: Is the aryl chloride activated enough?
Does base destroy the boronic acid?
Is air or water beneficial for this ligand system?

reactionSystems.name: Buchwald-Hartwig amination
conditions: Bulky electron-rich phosphine ligand
strong base
dry degassed solvent in many protocols
nextQuestions: Should a milder base be screened?
Does the amine salt need free-basing?

reactionSystems.name: Sonogashira coupling
conditions: Pd catalyst
amine base
copper co-catalyst or copper-free variant

reactionSystems.name: Mizoroki-Heck olefination
conditions: Base
polar aprotic solvent
ligand and temperature matched to halide
nextQuestions: Does the substrate tolerate high temperature?

reactionSystems.name: Amide coupling workflow
conditions: carbodiimide, uronium or imidazolide activation
base matched to amine salt
water exclusion when needed
nextQuestions: Would CDI or acid chloride be cleaner?

reactionSystems.name: Reductive amination
reactionSystems.className: C-N bond formation through imine chemistry
conditions: mild acid buffer
imine or iminium formation
chemoselective hydride donor

reactionSystems.name: Wittig and HWE olefination
conditions: ylide or phosphonate base generation
temperature and counter-ion control
aldehyde usually more reactive than ketone

reactionSystems.name: Aldol-Michael annulation logic
conditions: base or organocatalyst
temperature-controlled enolate formation
acceptor electrophilicity

reactionSystems.name: Grignard carbonyl addition
conditions: dry ether
slow electrophile addition
acidic work-up
nextQuestions: Which functional groups must be protected?

caseStudies.title: Unknown carbonyl assignment
caseStudies.question: Does the evidence support assigning the unknown as an aldehyde rather than a ketone?
caseStudies.thesis: The combined IR, 1H NMR and 13C NMR pattern supports an aldehyde assignment, with the strongest single observation being the downfield 1H signal near 9.8 ppm.

observations.inference: Consistent with a carbonyl functional group.
observations.limitation: The band alone does not distinguish aldehyde from ketone, ester or acid.

observations.inference: Strong evidence for an aldehydic proton.
observations.limitation: Confirm integration and rule out impurity or solvent artefact.

observations.inference: Consistent with aldehyde or ketone carbonyl carbon.
observations.limitation: Requires correlation with proton NMR for aldehyde-specific assignment.

observations.observation: Assignment uses converging spectral evidence rather than one diagnostic peak.
observations.inference: The claim has stronger academic defensibility.
observations.limitation: Reference spectra or authentic sample comparison would increase confidence.

argument.claim: The unknown contains an aldehyde carbonyl.
argument.warrant: Aldehydes commonly show both a carbonyl signal and a distinctive downfield proton resonance; a ketone would lack the aldehydic 1H signal.
argument.counter: A single IR carbonyl absorption is insufficient because many carbonyl derivatives absorb in similar regions.
argument.nextTest: Acquire 2D NMR or compare against a reference spectrum from an evaluated database.

report.aim: Assign the most plausible carbonyl class from the available spectral data.
report.method: Compare observed IR, 1H NMR and 13C NMR features against curated reference ranges and record limitations.
report.conclusion: The spectral pattern supports aldehyde assignment, with the aldehydic proton providing the discriminating evidence.
report.limitations: No authentic sample comparison, no 2D NMR, and no purity assessment are included in this static case file.

caseStudies.title: Hydride selectivity audit
caseStudies.question: Which reducing agent is academically defensible for reducing an aldehyde in the presence of an ester?
caseStudies.thesis: NaBH4 is the better first proposal when selective aldehyde or ketone reduction is desired, while LiAlH4 is too broad for an ester-containing substrate without protecting-group strategy.

observations.observation: NaBH4 is commonly treated as a milder hydride donor in teaching-lab selectivity problems.
observations.inference: It is a plausible selective reagent for aldehyde or ketone reduction.
observations.limitation: Actual chemoselectivity depends on substrate, solvent, temperature and work-up.

observations.observation: LiAlH4 can reduce esters and carboxylic acids under dry conditions.
observations.inference: It risks reducing the ester functionality.
observations.limitation: Procedure-specific evidence should be checked before final synthesis planning.

observations.observation: A milder, more selective reagent can reduce downstream purification and waste.
observations.inference: Selectivity is not only a yield question; it affects green chemistry metrics.
observations.limitation: A real green metric requires mass, solvent and waste accounting.

argument.claim: NaBH4 is the preferred initial reagent for selective carbonyl reduction in this scenario.
argument.warrant: A narrower functional-group scope is better aligned with preserving the ester functionality.
argument.counter: If the aldehyde is unusually hindered or deactivated, the mild reagent may be insufficient.
argument.nextTest: Check a substrate-specific procedure and run a small-scale monitored reaction if institutionally approved.

report.aim: Select a reducing agent that maximizes chemoselectivity and minimizes avoidable functional-group damage.
report.method: Compare reagent scope, compatibility and academic evidence level before choosing reaction conditions.
report.conclusion: NaBH4 is the defensible first proposal; LiAlH4 should be reserved for broader reductions or different targets.
report.limitations: This is a planning argument, not a validated experimental procedure.

caseStudies.title: Aromatic bromination mechanism audit
caseStudies.question: Why is a Lewis acid catalyst included for benzene bromination?
caseStudies.thesis: The Lewis acid increases electrophile strength, allowing substitution while preserving aromaticity after deprotonation.

observations.inference: Direct addition is disfavoured because it disrupts aromatic stabilization.
observations.limitation: Aromaticity is a model with several criteria, not a single measured value.

observations.inference: A stronger electrophilic brominating species is generated.
observations.limitation: The exact reactive species depends on conditions.

observations.inference: Substitution is favoured over permanent addition.

argument.claim: The Lewis acid is required to generate a sufficiently reactive electrophile for electrophilic aromatic substitution.
argument.warrant: Benzene's delocalized system is stable, so bromine must be activated before the ring attacks.
argument.counter: Highly activated aromatic rings may brominate under milder conditions than benzene.
argument.nextTest: Compare reaction rate or product distribution across activated and deactivated aromatic substrates.

report.aim: Explain the catalytic role in benzene bromination using electrophile strength and aromaticity.
report.method: Map each step to an evidence statement: electrophile generation, sigma complex formation and rearomatization.
report.conclusion: The catalyst enables electrophilic substitution by activating bromine while the mechanism ultimately restores aromaticity.
report.limitations: This is a mechanistic explanation and not a full kinetic or computational study.

caseStudies.title: Green metric route comparison
caseStudies.question: How should a route be evaluated when yield and hazard point in different directions?
caseStudies.thesis: A more academic comparison reports yield, selectivity, waste boundary and hazard rather than ranking routes by percentage yield alone.

observations.observation: Yield measures desired product relative to theoretical amount.
observations.inference: Yield does not account for solvent, auxiliary reagents or purification burden.
observations.limitation: Yield remains important, but it is incomplete.

observations.observation: E-factor considers waste mass relative to product mass.
observations.inference: A lower E-factor can identify a cleaner route even when yields are similar.

observations.observation: Hazardous reagents and solvents change the risk profile.
observations.inference: Academic route choice should include safety and sustainability language.
observations.limitation: Full assessment requires SDS and institutional controls.

argument.claim: Route evaluation should combine yield, selectivity, hazard and waste metrics.
argument.warrant: A single percentage yield can hide poor atom economy, hazardous conditions or heavy purification waste.
argument.counter: At discovery scale, data may be incomplete; report assumptions instead of overclaiming.
argument.nextTest: Calculate yield, atom economy and E-factor with clearly defined mass boundaries.

report.aim: Compare routes using a defensible sustainability framework.
report.method: State yield, waste boundary, hazardous inputs, purification burden and evidence level for each claim.
report.conclusion: The academically stronger route argument is multi-criterion, not yield-only.
report.limitations: The static example does not include full inventory masses or SDS-specific hazard coding.

checklist: Claim is stated as a testable chemical assertion.
Evidence type and evidence level are recorded.
At least one limitation or counterargument is included.
Terminology is consistent with IUPAC-style usage where relevant.
Numerical claims include units and context.
Sources are linked to the claim rather than listed as decoration.

dossiers.title: Carbonyl Forensics Dossier
dossiers.abstract: A structured case file for distinguishing aldehydes, ketones and acyl derivatives by converging IR, 1H NMR, 13C NMR and mass-spectral evidence.
observables: IR carbonyl region between 1680 and 1750 cm-1
claims: Aldehyde assignment requires evidence beyond an IR carbonyl band.
Converging spectra are stronger than a single diagnostic peak.
Reference comparison should include solvent, concentration and purity context.
limitations: No two-dimensional NMR is embedded in the static prototype.
Reference ranges are pedagogic summaries rather than full spectra.
Authentic sample comparison remains the strongest confirmatory step.

dossiers.title: Hydride Selectivity Dossier
dossiers.abstract: A chemoselectivity audit that compares NaBH4 and LiAlH4 using scope, functional-group tolerance, work-up burden and evidence level.
claims: A narrower reagent scope can be academically preferable when selectivity is the target.
LiAlH4 is stronger than many first-pass aldehyde or ketone reductions require.
A reagent recommendation should name the condition assumptions.
limitations: Substrate-specific literature is still required for real synthesis planning.
Stereochemical and protecting-group cases are simplified.
Safety data must be checked outside the prototype before lab use.

dossiers.title: Aromatic Substitution Dossier
dossiers.abstract: A mechanism-first dossier for electrophilic aromatic substitution, connecting electrophile generation, sigma-complex stability, directing effects and loss of aromaticity.
claims: Benzene bromination requires electrophile activation in standard teaching examples.
Restoration of aromaticity explains substitution rather than permanent addition.
Substituent effects must be separated from reagent strength.
limitations: No kinetic dataset is included for comparing substituted rings.
Reactive electrophile identity can vary with conditions.
The dossier is a reasoning tool rather than a full computational study.

dossiers.title: Green Route Assessment Dossier
dossiers.abstract: A route-evaluation dossier that treats yield, waste boundary, hazard, solvent burden and purification load as separate claims.
observables: Mass of waste included in boundary
claims: Yield alone is not a sufficient route-quality argument.
E-factor claims require an explicitly stated mass boundary.
A lower hazard route can be academically stronger even when yield is similar.
limitations: No full inventory spreadsheet is connected yet.
SDS-coded hazard scoring is represented narratively.
Life-cycle assessment is outside the current static scope.

methods.name: Spectral triangulation
methods.note: Assign structure only when independent spectral features point to the same chemical class.

methods.name: Evidence claim ladder
methods.note: Separate reference data, checked procedures, formal terminology and teaching heuristics.

methods.name: Condition register
methods.note: Record solvent, atmosphere, work-up, scale and substrate assumptions before making route claims.

methods.name: Limitation audit
methods.note: Every strong claim should carry a boundary, counterexample or next experiment.

reviewQueue.item: Attach raw spectra to Carbonyl Forensics
Add source-specific procedure links to Hydride Selectivity
Expand EAS examples with directing-effect cases
Add inventory table for Green Route Assessment

protocols.title: Claim Audit Protocol
protocols.summary: Converts an informal chemistry statement into a testable claim with evidence type, source family, limitation and next validation step.
checklist: The claim is written as a chemical assertion rather than a topic heading.
A source family is attached to the evidence, not just listed at the end.
A limitation is included before the conclusion is strengthened.
Units, solvent, phase, temperature or substrate class are recorded where relevant.
protocols.example: Do not write 'PCC oxidizes alcohols.' Write 'PCC is commonly used for selective oxidation of many primary alcohols to aldehydes under anhydrous textbook conditions; substrate-specific procedure evidence is still required.'

protocols.title: Spectral Triangulation Protocol
protocols.summary: Ranks a structural assignment by the number and independence of supporting spectral observations.
checklist: At least two independent observations support the same functional group.
A diagnostic peak is not treated as complete structure proof.
Integration, multiplicity and chemical shift are interpreted together.
Impurity, solvent and concentration artefacts are considered.
protocols.example: A carbonyl IR band supports a carbonyl class; an aldehydic 1H signal and 13C carbonyl resonance make the aldehyde claim stronger.

protocols.title: Route Comparison Protocol
protocols.summary: Compares routes by selectivity, hazard, waste boundary, work-up burden, evidence level and operational simplicity.
checklist: Yield is not used as the only route-quality metric.
Hazard and work-up burden are stated separately from selectivity.
Boundary assumptions are listed before an E-factor or waste claim.
Procedure-specific evidence is requested before lab-scale recommendation.
protocols.example: A lower-yield route may be academically preferred if it avoids a severe hazard and reduces purification waste.

protocols.title: Mechanism Discrimination Protocol
protocols.summary: Uses substrate structure, solvent, stereochemistry, rate law and product distribution to choose between plausible mechanisms.
checklist: A mechanism is treated as a constrained hypothesis rather than a memorized label.
Rate-law evidence is separated from stereochemical evidence.
Competing pathways are named when the same conditions could support them.
The weakest assumption is explicitly identified.
protocols.example: A primary substrate and strong nucleophile support SN2, but solvent and steric context still matter.

protocols.title: Reproducibility Ledger Protocol
protocols.summary: Tracks whether a claim has raw data, metadata, source link, uncertainty note and repeatability statement.
checklist: Raw observation and processed interpretation are separated.
Instrument, solvent or method metadata are present.
Missing data are marked as missing rather than silently omitted.
A review action is created for every incomplete record.
protocols.example: A spectral assignment without raw spectra can be useful for learning, but it should not be presented as publication-ready evidence.

rubric.name: Primary measurement
rubric.standard: Raw or evaluated numerical evidence with units, context and method metadata.

rubric.name: Checked procedure
rubric.standard: Peer-reviewed or independently checked experimental procedure with characterization.

rubric.name: Formal terminology
rubric.standard: Definition anchored in a controlled terminology source.

rubric.name: Heuristic
rubric.standard: Teaching rule or planning pattern with explicit limits and mechanism rationale.

rubric.name: Speculation
rubric.standard: Plausible but currently unsupported statement requiring a named validation step.

manuscriptSections.title: Abstract
manuscriptSections.purpose: State the chemical problem, method and main conclusion without overstating the evidence.
manuscriptSections.qualitySignal: Contains one defensible claim and one boundary condition.

manuscriptSections.title: Methods
manuscriptSections.purpose: Record conditions, data source, instrument context, calculation assumptions and selection criteria.
manuscriptSections.qualitySignal: Another reader can identify what was measured or compared.

manuscriptSections.title: Evidence
manuscriptSections.purpose: Separate observations from inferences so the argument can be audited.
manuscriptSections.qualitySignal: Each inference points back to a specific observation.

manuscriptSections.title: Limitations
manuscriptSections.purpose: Show where the claim stops and what further evidence would strengthen it.
manuscriptSections.qualitySignal: At least one plausible counterargument is named.

manuscriptSections.title: Conclusion
manuscriptSections.purpose: Resolve the research question using evidence strength rather than rhetorical certainty.
manuscriptSections.qualitySignal: Confidence matches the weakest required evidence item.

cases.title: Aldehyde vs ketone discrimination
cases.question: Does the combined spectrum support an aldehyde rather than a ketone?
cases.conclusion: The aldehydic 1H resonance is the discriminating observation; IR alone is insufficient.
cases.missing: 2D NMR or authentic reference comparison would strengthen the assignment.

cases.title: Ester hydrolysis progress check
cases.question: Do the spectra suggest conversion of an ester toward acid or alcohol products?

signals.interpretation: Carboxylic acid O-H may be emerging

cases.conclusion: The evidence is consistent with hydrolysis progress but needs time-course or reference comparison.
cases.missing: Quantitative integration and isolated product characterization.

cases.title: Halogen isotope pattern audit
cases.question: Does the molecular ion region support chlorine or bromine substitution?

signals.interpretation: Bromine is more likely than chlorine
Other spectra constrain structure but do not replace isotope evidence

cases.conclusion: A near 1:1 M/M+2 pattern is strong evidence for bromine, subject to molecular-ion assignment.

cases.title: Aromatic substitution pattern screen
cases.question: Can the aromatic region support a substituted benzene pattern?
cases.conclusion: The spectra support an aromatic ring but substitution pattern needs coupling analysis or 2D NMR.

instruments.name: FTIR
strengths: Strong for carbonyl and O-H/N-H regions
limitations: Functional group evidence is not complete structural proof
Sample preparation can affect band shape

instruments.name: 1H NMR
strengths: Integration and multiplicity give high information density
Downfield aldehyde and aromatic regions can be diagnostic
limitations: Exchangeable protons can broaden or disappear
Impurities and solvent peaks can mislead

instruments.name: 13C NMR
strengths: Carbonyl, aromatic and heteroatom-adjacent carbons are informative
limitations: Signal intensity is not directly quantitative in routine spectra

instruments.name: Mass spectrometry
strengths: Molecular ion and isotope patterns constrain formula
limitations: Molecular ion may be weak or absent
Isomers may share the same formula

uncertaintyRules: Prefer converging evidence from independent techniques.
Do not let one diagnostic signal erase conflicting observations.
Record the distinction between observed signal, interpretation and structural claim.
Escalate confidence only when missing metadata and artefact risks are addressed.

materials.name: Graphene oxide
materials.synthesis: Usually discussed as oxidative functionalization of graphite followed by exfoliation; reduction produces reduced graphene oxide with different conductivity and oxygen content.
limitations: GO is not a single stoichiometric compound
Residual oxidants and ions can affect measurements
materials.evidenceLevel: A material-family claim requires synthesis history, oxidation level and surface characterization.

materials.name: Reduced graphene oxide
materials.synthesis: Prepared by chemical, thermal or electrochemical reduction of graphene oxide.
limitations: Reduction does not fully restore pristine graphene
Performance comparisons need mass loading and morphology control
materials.evidenceLevel: Report reduction route and residual oxygen before claiming graphene-like behaviour.

materials.name: Carbon nanotubes
materials.synthesis: Commonly produced by catalytic chemical vapor deposition or arc/laser routes; purification and functionalization define usable material.
limitations: Single-wall and multi-wall CNTs are distinct material classes
materials.evidenceLevel: CNT claims should state wall type, purity, dispersion method and loading.

materials.name: Activated carbon
materials.synthesis: Generated by carbonization and physical or chemical activation of carbonaceous precursors.
limitations: Surface area alone does not predict adsorption
Ash and surface chemistry can dominate performance
materials.evidenceLevel: Adsorption claims require surface area, pore distribution and adsorption conditions.

materials.name: Silica gel
materials.synthesis: Prepared by silicate acidification or TEOS sol-gel hydrolysis and condensation.
limitations: Silanol density depends on thermal history
Particle size distribution matters in chromatography
materials.evidenceLevel: Silica claims require surface area, pore size and surface hydration context.

materials.name: Titanium dioxide
materials.synthesis: Produced by sol-gel hydrolysis of titanium alkoxides, hydrothermal methods or calcination of titanium salts.
limitations: Phase, crystallite size and surface area are entangled
Photocatalysis needs light spectrum and surface-state context
materials.evidenceLevel: Photocatalytic claims require phase, light source, surface area and degradation control experiments.

materials.name: Zinc oxide
materials.synthesis: Often prepared from zinc salts by precipitation, sol-gel processing, hydrothermal growth or thermal decomposition.
limitations: Defect chemistry changes electrical and optical behaviour
Particle morphology can dominate sensor response
Dopant and annealing history must be stated
materials.evidenceLevel: ZnO performance claims need morphology, phase and defect characterization.

materials.name: Magnetite nanoparticles
materials.synthesis: Commonly made by co-precipitating Fe(II)/Fe(III) salts under basic conditions, followed by coating or stabilization.
limitations: Aggregation changes magnetic and adsorption behaviour
Fe ratio and atmosphere control phase
materials.evidenceLevel: Magnetic claims require phase and magnetization data, not just black precipitate formation.

materials.name: MOF-5
materials.synthesis: Made from zinc salts and terephthalate linkers under solvothermal conditions in many literature examples.
limitations: Crystallinity does not guarantee open pores
materials.evidenceLevel: MOF claims require PXRD plus porosity after activation.

materials.name: ZIF-8
materials.synthesis: Prepared from zinc salts and 2-methylimidazole under solvothermal, room-temperature or aqueous conditions.
limitations: Gate-opening and diffusion effects complicate adsorption
Particle defects and residual linker affect performance
materials.evidenceLevel: ZIF-8 separation claims require adsorption and diffusion context.

materials.name: Nafion membrane
materials.synthesis: Commercial perfluorosulfonic acid ionomer processed as membrane or dispersion-cast film.
materials.evidenceLevel: Membrane claims need hydration state, thickness and conductivity method.

materials.name: PEDOT:PSS
materials.synthesis: Commercially supplied or prepared by oxidative polymerization of EDOT in the presence of PSS.
materials.evidenceLevel: Conductivity claims require film thickness, additive and annealing details.

materials.name: Polyaniline
materials.synthesis: Oxidative polymerization of aniline under acidic conditions is the common route.
materials.evidenceLevel: Report oxidation state, dopant acid and conductivity method.

materials.name: Polyacrylamide hydrogel
materials.synthesis: Radical polymerization of acrylamide with bisacrylamide crosslinker, often initiated by APS/TEMED.
limitations: Mechanical properties depend on crosslinker and water content
materials.evidenceLevel: Hydrogel claims need composition, crosslinker ratio and swelling state.

materials.name: Polylactic acid
materials.synthesis: Prepared by lactide ring-opening polymerization or condensation routes depending on target molecular weight.
materials.evidenceLevel: Sustainability claims require degradation conditions, molecular weight and stereochemical composition.

materials.name: Polydimethylsiloxane
materials.synthesis: Typically formed by curing vinyl-terminated PDMS with hydrosilane crosslinkers using platinum catalysts.
limitations: Small molecules can absorb into PDMS
materials.evidenceLevel: Device claims need cure ratio, surface treatment history and swelling/absorption context.

materials.name: Methylammonium lead iodide perovskite
materials.synthesis: Prepared by solution deposition or vapor-assisted conversion of lead iodide and methylammonium iodide precursors.
limitations: Lead toxicity and moisture instability are central
Atmosphere and encapsulation must be recorded
materials.evidenceLevel: Photovoltaic claims require device architecture, scan protocol, stability and encapsulation context.

materials.name: Lithium iron phosphate
materials.synthesis: Prepared by solid-state, hydrothermal or sol-gel routes followed by heat treatment and often carbon coating.
limitations: Electronic conductivity is low without modification
Particle size and carbon coating dominate performance
materials.evidenceLevel: Battery claims need electrode loading, electrolyte, cycling rate and voltage window.

materials.name: Manganese dioxide
materials.synthesis: Produced by redox precipitation, hydrothermal synthesis or electrochemical deposition.
limitations: Polymorph and hydration state determine performance
Conductive additive and binder affect electrochemical data
materials.evidenceLevel: Electrochemical claims need phase, mass loading and electrolyte details.

materials.name: Hydroxyapatite
materials.synthesis: Prepared by wet precipitation, hydrothermal synthesis, sol-gel methods or biomimetic mineralization.
limitations: Carbonate substitution and Ca/P ratio affect claims
materials.evidenceLevel: Biomedical claims need phase purity, ion ratio and biological testing context.

materials.name: Chitosan
materials.synthesis: Obtained by deacetylation of chitin; crosslinking and functionalization tune material properties.
materials.evidenceLevel: Chitosan claims need deacetylation degree, molecular weight and pH context.

materials.name: Calcium alginate hydrogel
materials.synthesis: Formed by exposing sodium alginate solution to calcium ions, creating egg-box crosslinking domains.
limitations: Gel stability depends on ionic strength
Mechanical properties depend on G/M block ratio
Sterility and purity matter for biological uses
materials.evidenceLevel: Hydrogel claims require alginate composition, calcium concentration and swelling medium.

materials.name: Poly(methyl methacrylate)
materials.synthesis: Prepared by free-radical polymerization of methyl methacrylate with thermal or photochemical initiators.
limitations: Molecular weight and tacticity affect properties
Residual monomer can bias biocompatibility claims
materials.evidenceLevel: Polymer claims need molecular weight, dispersity, additive content and thermal history.

materials.name: Poly(vinylidene fluoride)
materials.synthesis: Industrial fluoropolymer produced by vinylidene fluoride polymerization; processing controls crystalline phase.
limitations: Solvent casting can leave residual solvent
Binder claims need electrode formulation context
materials.evidenceLevel: PVDF records need phase, processing route and additive context.

materials.name: Polystyrene
materials.synthesis: Prepared by radical, anionic or controlled polymerization of styrene.
limitations: Thermal and mechanical claims depend on molecular weight
Foam behavior is not bulk polymer behavior
materials.evidenceLevel: State molecular weight, dispersity and architecture before comparing properties.

materials.name: Poly(ethylene glycol)
materials.synthesis: Produced by ethylene oxide polymerization or used as functionalized PEG derivatives.
materials.evidenceLevel: PEG claims need molecular weight, end-group identity and dispersity.

materials.name: ZSM-5 zeolite
materials.synthesis: Hydrothermal crystallization of silica and alumina sources with organic structure-directing agents.
materials.evidenceLevel: Zeolite claims need phase, Si/Al ratio, acidity and calcination history.

materials.name: Aluminium oxide
materials.synthesis: Produced by precipitation, calcination or sol-gel routes with phase determined by thermal history.
limitations: Gamma, alpha and other phases differ strongly
materials.evidenceLevel: Report phase, surface area, pore structure and pretreatment.

materials.name: Lithium lanthanum zirconium oxide
materials.synthesis: Prepared by solid-state reaction, sol-gel or tape-casting routes followed by high-temperature sintering.
materials.evidenceLevel: Battery electrolyte claims need phase, density, conductivity and interface protocol.

materials.name: Carbon black
materials.synthesis: Industrial incomplete-combustion material with grade-specific particle and aggregate structure.
materials.evidenceLevel: State grade, surface area, loading and dispersion method.

materials.name: UiO-66
materials.synthesis: Solvothermal assembly of zirconium salts with terephthalate linker, often using acid modulators to control nucleation and defects.
materials.evidenceLevel: A UiO-66 claim needs PXRD plus porosity or defect evidence, not just white powder formation.

materials.name: MIL-101(Cr)
materials.synthesis: Hydrothermal chromium-linker assembly followed by extensive washing and activation.
limitations: Activation protocol strongly affects surface area
materials.evidenceLevel: Report washing, activation and porosity before comparing adsorption capacity.

materials.name: Imine covalent organic framework
materials.synthesis: Dynamic covalent condensation between aldehyde and amine nodes under solvothermal or interfacial conditions.
materials.evidenceLevel: COF assignments require linkage evidence plus crystallinity and porosity.

materials.name: Ti3C2Tx MXene
materials.synthesis: Selective etching of MAX phase followed by delamination, washing and oxidation management.
materials.evidenceLevel: State etching system, washing endpoint and storage conditions for reproducibility.

materials.name: Graphitic carbon nitride
materials.synthesis: Thermal condensation of melamine, urea or dicyandiamide followed by exfoliation or doping when required.
materials.evidenceLevel: Photocatalysis needs light spectrum, control experiments and normalized surface area.

materials.name: Cellulose nanofiber
materials.synthesis: Mechanical fibrillation often aided by TEMPO oxidation, enzymatic treatment or acid pretreatment.
materials.evidenceLevel: Report fibrillation energy, surface charge and drying history.

materials.name: Aromatic polyimide
properties: Low dielectric loss in selected grades
materials.synthesis: Dianhydride and diamine polyamic acid formation followed by thermal or chemical imidization.
materials.evidenceLevel: Thermal and mechanical claims should include imidization protocol and film thickness.

materials.name: Epoxy-amine thermoset
materials.synthesis: Step-growth curing between multifunctional epoxides and amines under stoichiometric and thermal control.
materials.evidenceLevel: Cure schedule, stoichiometry and conversion are required before comparing properties.

materials.name: Sulfide solid electrolyte
materials.synthesis: Mechanochemical or solid-state routes using lithium sulfide and phosphorus sulfide precursors followed by annealing.
materials.evidenceLevel: Conductivity claims need pellet density, blocking electrodes and atmosphere history.

materials.name: NASICON-type solid electrolyte
materials.synthesis: Solid-state or sol-gel oxide route followed by high-temperature sintering and density optimization.
materials.evidenceLevel: Separate bulk and grain-boundary conductivity when possible.

materials.name: Prussian blue analog
materials.synthesis: Controlled precipitation from metal salts and hexacyanoferrate sources with chelation or slow-feed control.
materials.evidenceLevel: Report water and vacancy content for meaningful battery comparisons.

materials.name: Polydopamine coating
materials.synthesis: Oxidative polymerization of dopamine in weakly basic aqueous buffer on immersed substrates.
materials.evidenceLevel: A PDA coating claim needs surface-specific evidence and washing/leaching controls.

materials.name: PEG hydrogel
materials.synthesis: Photocrosslinking, Michael addition or click crosslinking of multi-arm PEG macromers.
limitations: weak mechanical strength at high swelling
materials.evidenceLevel: Report macromer functionality, crosslinking conversion and swelling medium.

materials.name: PNIPAM hydrogel
properties: LCST near aqueous room/body temperature range
materials.synthesis: Free-radical or controlled radical polymerization with crosslinker and aqueous precipitation options.
materials.evidenceLevel: Thermoresponse must be measured under stated salt, pH and concentration conditions.

materials.name: PDMS elastomer
materials.synthesis: Hydrosilylation curing of vinyl and hydride silicone prepolymers with platinum catalyst.
materials.evidenceLevel: PDMS device claims need cure ratio, surface treatment age and solvent exposure history.

materials.name: Methylammonium lead iodide perovskite
materials.synthesis: Solution deposition or vapor-assisted conversion of lead iodide and methylammonium iodide precursors.
materials.evidenceLevel: Device claims require scan protocol, encapsulation state and stability measurement.

materials.name: Layered NMC cathode
materials.synthesis: Co-precipitation of transition-metal hydroxide or carbonate followed by lithiation and calcination.
materials.evidenceLevel: Report composition, calcination, electrode loading and voltage window.

materials.name: Silicon-graphite composite anode
materials.synthesis: Mechanical blending, carbon coating or composite particle engineering with graphite and polymer binder.
materials.evidenceLevel: Normalize capacity to active mass and report pre-lithiation or additive strategy.

materials.name: Biochar adsorbent
materials.synthesis: Pyrolysis or hydrothermal carbonization followed by activation or surface oxidation when required.
characterization: pH at point of zero charge
materials.evidenceLevel: Biochar claims must specify feedstock, pyrolysis temperature and washing protocol.

characterizationMethods.purpose: Phase identity, crystallinity and lattice-level material comparison
Carbon disorder, vibrational fingerprints and conjugated material signatures
Surface elemental composition, oxidation state and functional-group evidence
Surface area, pore volume and pore-size distribution
Morphology, particle size, aggregation and nanoscale structure
Capacitance, conductivity, redox response and device-relevant performance
Thermal transitions, degradation and solvent or residue content
Separates surface oxidation state, heteroatom content and termination chemistry from bulk formula claims.
Distinguishes bulk, grain-boundary and interfacial resistance in ion conductors and electrochemical devices.
Maps glass transition, storage modulus and network relaxation for thermoset and elastomer systems.

sources.name: PubMed
sources.scope: peer-reviewed abstracts, MeSH-indexed biomedical chemistry, pharmacology, toxicology
sources.bestFor: mechanistic biology, medicinal chemistry context, toxicology, translational studies

sources.name: PubChem
sources.owner: NIH / National Center for Biotechnology Information
sources.scope: compound records, synonyms, identifiers, assay links, safety and vendor metadata
sources.bestFor: compound identity checks, CAS/synonym validation, bioassay starting points

sources.name: PubMed Central
sources.scope: open full-text biomedical and methods literature
sources.bestFor: protocol details, figures, supporting information, review-level background

sources.name: NCBI Bookshelf
sources.owner: NIH / National Center for Biotechnology Information
sources.scope: reference chapters, toxicology summaries, clinical and biochemical background
sources.bestFor: authoritative background when local notes are too brief

sources.name: NIST Chemistry WebBook
sources.owner: National Institute of Standards and Technology
sources.bestFor: IR/MS reference checks and physical property triangulation

sources.name: IUPAC Gold Book
sources.owner: International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry

sources.name: Crossref
sources.bestFor: citation discovery and DOI-level bibliographic checks

evidenceQueue.title: Do not call a MOF record complete from PXRD alone
evidenceQueue.action: Add activation, BET and solvent-exchange evidence before comparing adsorption.

evidenceQueue.title: Separate reaction name from mechanism proof
evidenceQueue.action: Attach kinetics, stereochemical outcome or control experiments to the named pathway.

evidenceQueue.title: Treat imported NIH/PubChem metadata as discovery
evidenceQueue.action: Show source links and identifiers, then keep local notes concise and citable.

evidenceQueue.title: Polymer claims need molar-mass data
evidenceQueue.action: Add Mn, dispersity, conversion and purification history.

evidenceQueue.title: Battery electrolyte claims need interfacial context
evidenceQueue.action: Document electrode loading, formation protocol, water content and impedance model.
